group.topic_path: 12. Platelets › Platelet Transfusions › Collection and Storage of Platelet Preparations/Concentrates

questions.stem: A regional blood center performs plateletpheresis procedures that typically take 45 to 90 minutes and yield the equivalent of 6 to 8 whole blood-derived platelet units from a single donor, with the platelets suspended in donor plasma inside a collection bag specifically designed for platelet storage. To reduce plasma-related adverse transfusion reactions and to make more plasma available for further manufacturing, the center begins storing a portion of these apheresis-collected units in platelet additive solution (PAS) instead of 100% plasma. The blood bank supervisor at an affiliated US hospital, hoping to standardize inventory, asks whether whole blood (WB)-derived platelet concentrates prepared in-house can also be converted to PAS storage.

options.text: PAS storage is currently approved in the United States for apheresis platelets only; platelets prepared from whole blood cannot be stored in PAS
PAS storage is currently approved in the United States for whole blood-derived platelets only; apheresis platelets must be stored in 100% plasma
PAS storage is currently approved in the United States equally for both apheresis platelets and whole blood-derived platelets
PAS storage is not approved for any platelet product in the United States regardless of collection method
Eligibility for PAS storage in the United States is determined by storage temperature rather than by collection method

questions.explanation_correct: The source states that in the United States, current PAS formulations are approved for storage of apheresis platelets only and cannot be used for the storage of platelets prepared from whole blood (WB). This is explicitly contrasted with Europe, where both apheresis and WB-derived platelets can be stored in either plasma or PAS. Therefore the hospital's WB-derived platelet concentrates cannot legally be converted to PAS storage under current US regulations, while the center's apheresis units may be.

option_rationales.text: Correct. The source explicitly states that in the US, current PAS formulations are approved for apheresis platelets only and cannot be used for platelets prepared from WB.
Incorrect. This reverses the actual restriction described in the source, which permits PAS storage for apheresis platelets, not WB-derived platelets, in the United States.
Incorrect. Equal approval for both product types describes the situation in Europe per the source, not the United States, where PAS is restricted to apheresis platelets.
Incorrect. PAS storage is not banned outright in the US; it is specifically permitted for apheresis-collected platelets.

Incorrect. The source ties PAS approval to the collection method (apheresis vs WB), not to storage temperature; storage below 20 C is instead associated with negative effects on platelet recovery and survival, an unrelated storage-temperature concern.

source_references.supports: US restriction of PAS storage to apheresis-collected platelets, exclusion of whole blood-derived platelets, and contrast with European practice allowing both

source_references.source_id: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices
source_references.supports: Details of apheresis platelet collection: yield equivalent to 6-8 whole blood-derived units, platelets suspended in donor plasma in a bag designed for platelet storage, and a typical 45-90 minute procedure time

questions.teaching_point: In the United States, platelet additive solution (PAS) is currently FDA-approved for storage of apheresis-collected platelets only; whole blood-derived platelet concentrates must still be stored in 100% plasma. This is a key regulatory distinction from European practice, where PAS may be used for both apheresis and WB-derived platelets.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with no history of transfusion, transplantation, or injection of blood products has an unexpected antibody detected during pretransfusion antibody screening. The antibody reacts most strongly at room temperature (with weaker reactivity at 37°C) and shows evidence of complement activation in vitro. Which of the following best explains the most likely origin and immunoglobulin class of this antibody?

options.text: It is an IgG antibody induced by prior sensitization from transfusion of foreign RBC antigens
It is a monoclonal antibody produced from a single clonally expanded B-cell line, as used in reagent antisera
It is a naturally occurring IgM antibody, likely induced by environmental substances (such as pollens or bacterial membrane components) that structurally resemble RBC antigens
It reacts optimally at 37°C, consistent with sensitization from a prior pregnancy
It is most likely directed against a Kell system antigen, given the high immunogenicity of Kell antigens relative to other blood group systems

questions.explanation_correct: The patient has no history of exposure to foreign RBC antigens (no transfusion, transplantation, or pregnancy), and the antibody's serologic behavior—optimal reactivity at or below room temperature with complement activation—matches the described profile of naturally occurring antibodies. These are considered to arise from environmental exposure to substances (e.g., pollen grains, bacterial membranes) that structurally resemble RBC antigens, and most are IgM cold agglutinins that react best at room temperature or lower, fix complement, and can be hemolytic when active at 37°C.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. Immune antibodies (typically IgG) arise after sensitization by transfusion, transplantation, or pregnancy exposure to foreign RBC antigens—none of which this patient has experienced—and this antibody's room-temperature/complement-activating behavior does not fit the typical warm-reactive IgG pattern of immune antibodies.

Incorrect. Monoclonal antibodies refer to laboratory reagent antisera manufactured from a single clonally expanded B-cell line for use in blood typing reagents; they are not a description of antibodies produced naturally by a patient's own immune system.

Correct. This scenario (no prior antigen exposure, room-temperature reactivity, complement activation) matches the described characteristics of naturally occurring IgM antibodies, thought to be induced by environmental substances resembling RBC antigens.

Incorrect. Reactivity optimal at 37°C together with a history of pregnancy sensitization describes an immune antibody pattern, not the naturally occurring, room-temperature-reactive antibody described in this scenario, and this patient has no pregnancy history.

Incorrect. The common naturally occurring antibodies described react with antigens of the ABH, Hh, Ii, Lewis, MN, and P blood group systems—Kell is not listed among the systems targeted by naturally occurring antibodies in this context.

source_references.locator: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.61
source_references.supports: Describes naturally occurring antibodies as found in individuals never exposed to RBC antigens via transfusion, injection, or pregnancy; likely produced in response to environmental substances (pollen, bacterial membranes) resembling RBC antigens; most are IgM cold agglutinins reacting best at room temperature or lower, activating complement, and potentially hemolytic at 37°C; common naturally occurring antibodies target ABH, Hh, Ii, Lewis, MN, and P systems. Also distinguishes monoclonal (reagent) antibodies as derived from a single clonally expanded B-cell line.

questions.teaching_point: Naturally occurring antibodies (commonly IgM, cold-reactive, complement-activating, arising without known RBC antigen exposure, typically against ABH/Hh/Ii/Lewis/MN/P systems) are distinct from immune antibodies (typically IgG, warm-reactive, arising after transfusion/transplantation/pregnancy) and from monoclonal reagent antibodies (manufactured antisera from a single B-cell clone).

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with aplastic anemia is being supported with apheresis platelet transfusions from her HLA-matched sibling, who serves as a repeat (directed) apheresis platelet donor specifically for her over several months to limit further alloimmunization. The donor completed a full pre-donation testing panel (ABO group/Rh type, antibody screen, and infectious disease markers) with his first donation for this patient. He now presents to donate again for this same recipient 3 weeks later. According to standard blood bank practice for a donor donating repeatedly for a specific intended recipient, what is the minimum interval at which the donor's ABO/Rh type, antibody screen, and infectious disease marker testing must be repeated?

options.text: Every 4 weeks
Every 30 days
Every 8 weeks
Every 4 months
Only on the donor's first donation to this recipient; no repeat testing is required thereafter

questions.explanation_correct: For apheresis platelet donors, testing requirements are the same as for other allogeneic blood products (ABO group/Rh type, antibody screen, and infectious disease markers). When the same donor is donating repeatedly for a specific intended recipient (a directed/repeat donation situation), the source text specifies that repeat testing need only be performed every 30 days, rather than at every single donation.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. A 4-week interval is the threshold in the source text for requiring a platelet count (must be >150,000/uL if the interval between donations is less than 4 weeks), not the testing repeat interval for ABO/Rh, antibody screen, or infectious disease markers.
Correct. The passage states that when a donor is donating repeatedly for a specific patient, repeat testing (ABO/Rh, antibody screen, infectious disease markers) need only be done every 30 days.

Incorrect. An 8-week deferral applies when the donor has donated whole blood, or when 100 mL or more of red cells could not be returned during a prior apheresis procedure, to allow RBC mass replenishment; this is a donation-frequency deferral, not a testing-repeat interval.

Incorrect. Every 4 months is the interval at which FDA guidelines require a physician to review the donation records of regular plateletpheresis donors, not the interval for repeating ABO/Rh, antibody screen, or infectious disease testing.

Incorrect. Testing is not limited to only the first donation; the source specifies an ongoing repeat-testing requirement (at least every 30 days) even for a donor giving repeatedly to the same recipient.

source_references.supports: States apheresis platelet donor testing requirements are the same as other allogeneic products (ABO group/Rh type, antibody screen, infectious disease markers), and that for a donor donating repeatedly for a specific patient, repeat testing need only be done every 30 days; also provides the 4-week platelet count threshold, the 8-week RBC replenishment deferral, and the 4-month physician record review interval used as distractors.

questions.stem: A hospital transfusion service performs a Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) of its pretransfusion (type and screen) specimen collection process. The workflow is broken into sequential steps, including: the patient care technician identifies the patient by name and medical record number, collects the blood sample, and only then assembles the red identification arm band, labels the tube, and completes the transfusion requisition — all while still at the patient's bedside and in the patient's presence — before finally applying the red band to the patient. Which of the following best explains the primary safety rationale for labeling the tube and transfusion requisition in the presence of the patient immediately after the blood draw, rather than pre-labeling before the draw?

options.text: It ensures the labeled specimen is unambiguously linked to the patient who was just identified and bled, preventing a wrong-blood-in-tube (mislabeling) event before the sample reaches pretransfusion testing
It fulfills the FMEA team's recommendation that the nurse check the blood at the patient's bedside per Patient Care Policy
It reduces the need for increased auditing of transfusions on nursing units, which was recommended as a deterrent to the pretransfusion "desk check"
It removes the need for an electronic bar-coding system for specimen collection, since bedside manual labeling is an equally effective control
It confirms that the patient's ABO/Rh type on the current sample matches records from a previous encounter before the specimen is released to the laboratory

questions.explanation_correct: In the FMEA of the specimen collection process, the technician identifies the patient by name and medical record number, draws the sample, and only then labels the tube and requisition and applies the ID band, all before leaving the bedside. Sequencing labeling after collection and identification, and doing so in the patient's presence, ties the labeled tube directly to the specific patient who was just confirmed and bled, which is the core control against a mislabeled (wrong-blood-in-tube) specimen entering compatibility testing.

option_rationales.text: Correct: bedside labeling immediately after collection, in the patient's presence, is the control step that links the drawn sample to the verified patient and prevents specimen mislabeling.

Incorrect: the recommendation that a nurse check blood at the bedside per policy was the corrective action tied to the patient-identification step of the FMEA, a separate control from the act of labeling the tube itself.

Incorrect: increased auditing of transfusions as a deterrent to the "desk check" was recommended to address the step where the technician returns the completed transfusion requisition to the nurse, not the bedside labeling step.
Incorrect: the FMEA recommended an electronic bar-coding system as an additional control at a different process step, not as something rendered unnecessary by manual bedside labeling.
Incorrect: bedside labeling addresses correct specimen-to-patient linkage at collection; it does not itself verify or confirm the patient's ABO/Rh type against prior records.

source_references.supports: Describes the 13-step FMEA of the specimen collection process for type and screen, including patient identification by name/medical record number, blood collection, and labeling the arm band, tube, and requisition in the patient's presence before applying the red band.
FMEA Action Items: for the 'patient care technician identifies patient' step, the recommendation was that the nurse checks blood at the bedside per Patient Care Policy.

FMEA Action Items: for the step where the technician returns the transfusion requisition to the nurse, recommendations were implementing an electronic bar-coding system and increasing auditing of transfusions as a deterrent to the pretransfusion 'desk check.'

questions.teaching_point: In pretransfusion (compatibility) testing, positive patient identification and bedside specimen labeling — performed in the patient's presence immediately after collection, not before — is a critical control point against wrong-blood-in-tube errors, distinct from other quality controls like bedside product checks or requisition-handling audits.

group.topic_path: 3. RBCs and RBC Components › Carbohydrate Blood Groups and Blood Group Systems › ABH Antigens › Antigenic Variants

questions.stem: A blood donor's RBCs are not agglutinated, or are agglutinated only very weakly, by both anti-A and anti-A,B reagents on routine ABO forward typing. Adsorption and elution studies performed with anti-A give a strongly positive result, confirming that A antigen is present on the RBCs, and antigen quantitation shows an estimated 200 to 1,900 A antigen sites per RBC. Testing of the donor's serum demonstrates detectable A glycosyltransferase (A enzyme) activity of either the A1 or A2 type. The donor is a secretor, and saliva studies show normal quantities of both A and H substances. Which ABO subgroup best explains this constellation of findings?

options.text: A3
Aend
Am
Ax
Ay

questions.explanation_correct: The Am subgroup is characterized by RBCs that are not agglutinated, or only weakly agglutinated, by anti-A or anti-A,B, with A antigen presence confirmed by a strongly positive adsorption-elution study; the estimated number of A antigen sites in Am individuals ranges from 200 to 1,900 per RBC. Am is distinguished from the other A subgroups by two features described in the source: an A enzyme of either the A1 or A2 type is detectable in the serum, and secretor saliva contains normal quantities of both A and H substance (Am individuals also usually do not produce anti-A1). This combination of a low-antigen-density RBC phenotype together with a normally functioning, detectable serum transferase and normal saliva A/H substance is unique to Am among the subgroups listed.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. A3 is described in the source as very heterogeneous at the molecular level, with anti-A1 sometimes present in serum and A substance detectable in the saliva of A3 secretors, but it is not characterized in the passage by the adsorption-elution requirement, specific low antigen-site count, or detectable serum A-transferase described in this vignette.

Incorrect. Aend RBCs show very weak mixed-field agglutination with some anti-A and anti-A,B reagents rather than the pattern described here, no A glycosyltransferase is detectable in the serum, and secretor studies detect only H substance (not normal A and H substance) in the saliva of Aend secretors.

Incorrect. Ax RBCs are characteristically not agglutinated by anti-A but do agglutinate with most examples of anti-A,B; A transferase is not usually detectable in the serum of Ax individuals, and routine secretor studies detect only H substance in Ax saliva, with A substance detectable only by special agglutination/inhibition studies using Ax cells as indicators.

Incorrect. Ay RBCs require adsorption and elution to confirm A antigen, similar to Am, but eluate activity is characteristically weaker than that from Am RBCs, only trace amounts of A glycosyltransferase are detectable in serum, and saliva A substance is present in below-normal (not normal) quantities.

source_references.supports: Describes the serologic, biochemical, and secretor characteristics distinguishing the Am, Ax, Aend, Ay, and A3 ABO antigenic subgroup variants, including antigen site density, adsorption-elution requirements, serum A-transferase detectability, and saliva A/H substance quantities.
source_references.locator: p.132 (Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices)

questions.teaching_point: ABH antigenic variants (weak A subgroups such as Am, Ax, Aend, Ay, A3) are differentiated not by a single test but by a composite serologic/biochemical profile: RBC agglutination pattern with anti-A and anti-A,B, need for adsorption-elution to demonstrate antigen, presence/absence and type of serum A-transferase, and quantity of A/H substance in secretor saliva.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with long-standing chronic kidney disease and associated chronic anemia is seen in nephrology clinic. He is asymptomatic, hemodynamically stable, and not actively bleeding, with no history of cardiac or cerebral ischemic disease. His hemoglobin has remained stable in the range of 7.0-7.2 g/dL for the past several months, and his heart rate and cardiac output appear to have adapted to compensate for the low hemoglobin. Which of the following best reflects current evidence-based principles guiding the RBC transfusion decision for this patient?

options.text: Because his hemoglobin is at or below 7 g/dL, transfusion should be ordered now, since reaching a numeric threshold is itself an indication to transfuse.
RBCs should be transfused to raise the hemoglobin toward the traditional liberal range of 9-10 g/dL, which remains the safer standard of care.
Because he has chronic kidney disease, he requires maintenance of hemoglobin above 7.5-8 g/dL, the threshold reserved for patients with cardiovascular disease.

Transfusion should be withheld for now; chronic anemia is often well tolerated through physiologic adaptation, and a hemoglobin threshold should inform rather than dictate the decision, which should be individualized.
The decision should be based solely on his single lowest recorded (nadir) hemoglobin value, since this is the best predictor of adverse outcomes in chronically anemic patients.

questions.explanation_correct: The source text explicitly distinguishes 'threshold' from 'trigger': a trigger implies action should automatically be taken, whereas a threshold more broadly implies a value at which one could consider action. It further states that an arbitrary hemoglobin level should not be the sole driver of transfusion decisions, which should instead be individualized based on the patient's overall status. Patients with chronic anemia from chronic renal failure characteristically adapt physiologically to a lower hemoglobin by increasing cardiac output, heart rate, or stroke volume, and chronic anemia is much better tolerated than acute anemia. This asymptomatic, hemodynamically stable patient with long-standing, physiologically compensated anemia is exactly the scenario in which the hemoglobin value should inform, not automatically dictate, the transfusion decision.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect: this treats the hemoglobin threshold as an automatic 'trigger.' The source explicitly prefers the term 'threshold' over 'trigger' because a threshold is a value at which one could consider action, not one that mandates it; the decision must still be individualized.

Incorrect: multiple large randomized trials cited in the source compared restrictive thresholds (7-8 g/dL) to liberal thresholds (9-10 g/dL) and found outcomes were equivalent in most studies and worse with liberal transfusion in several others, so liberal transfusion is not the safer default.

Incorrect: the higher 7.5-8 g/dL threshold in the source applies specifically to patients with cardiovascular disease, which this patient explicitly does not have; chronic renal failure is instead cited as an example of anemia that is well tolerated at lower hemoglobin levels due to physiologic adaptation.
Correct, as detailed in explanation_correct.

Incorrect: the source states that the change in hemoglobin (delta hemoglobin) is a better predictor of adverse outcomes than the absolute nadir hemoglobin value, the opposite of what this option claims.

source_references.supports: Chronic anemia (e.g., from chronic renal failure) is often physiologically well tolerated via increased cardiac output, heart rate, or stroke volume; delta hemoglobin predicts outcomes better than nadir hemoglobin; an arbitrary hemoglobin threshold should not be the sole driver of transfusion decisions, which should be individualized.

source_references.source_id: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices
source_references.supports: 'Threshold' is preferred over 'trigger' because trigger implies action should be taken while threshold implies a value at which one could consider action; AABB guidance cites a 7 g/dL hemoglobin threshold for most patients and a higher threshold for those with cardiac conditions.

Nine large randomized trials support a restrictive RBC transfusion threshold of 7 g/dL (7.5-8 g/dL for cardiovascular disease) compared with liberal thresholds of 9-10 g/dL, with equivalent or worse outcomes seen using liberal thresholds.

questions.teaching_point: Restrictive hemoglobin thresholds (~7 g/dL, or 7.5-8 g/dL for cardiovascular disease) are supported by strong randomized-trial evidence, but 'threshold' denotes a value prompting consideration of transfusion rather than an automatic mandate ('trigger'); decisions must be individualized, accounting for chronicity/physiologic adaptation, hemodynamic status, and trend (delta) rather than an isolated hemoglobin number.

group.topic_path: 5. Apheresis › Specialized Therapeutic Hemapheresis and Phlebotomy › Therapeutic Phlebotomy › Polycythemia Vera

questions.stem: A 61-year-old man with JAK2 V617F-positive polycythemia vera is referred to the therapeutic apheresis service. His hematologist requests that his hematocrit be lowered using erythrocytapheresis rather than serial manual phlebotomy alone. The apheresis medical director is asked to classify this indication using the standard ASFA (American Society for Apheresis) indications table for therapeutic cytapheresis. Which of the following correctly identifies the ASFA category and expected treatment schedule for this indication?

options.text: ASFA Category II; leukocytapheresis performed daily (QD) with a variable number of treatments
ASFA Category II; thrombocytapheresis performed daily (QD) until the platelet count normalizes
ASFA Category I; erythrocytapheresis performed with a variable, individualized number of treatments
ASFA Category III; adsorptive cytapheresis performed weekly for five to ten treatments

questions.explanation_correct: The reference indications table for therapeutic cytapheresis lists 'polycythemia vera; erythrocytosis' (modifying condition: polycythemia vera) as an ASFA Category I indication, with erythrocytapheresis as the procedure and a variable (individualized) typical treatment course rather than a fixed interval or session count. This differs from other entries in the same table, which specify different procedures and are assigned Category II or III with defined schedules.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. Daily (QD) leukocytapheresis at ASFA Category II with a variable course is the entry listed for symptomatic hyperleukocytosis, not polycythemia vera, which uses a different procedure (erythrocytapheresis) and category (I).

Incorrect. Thrombocytapheresis performed daily (QD) at ASFA Category II is listed for symptomatic thrombocytosis. Although the source notes marked thrombocytosis (>1,000,000/µL) can occur in polycythemia vera, the procedure/category assigned to polycythemia vera itself is erythrocytapheresis at Category I, not thrombocytapheresis at Category II.
Correct. The table lists polycythemia vera/erythrocytosis as ASFA Category I, procedure erythrocytapheresis, typical course 'Variable' (individualized number of treatments).

Incorrect. Weekly adsorptive cytapheresis for five to ten treatments at ASFA Category III is the entry listed for inflammatory bowel disease (ulcerative colitis/Crohn disease), a different procedure, category, and indication than polycythemia vera.

source_references.supports: The ASFA indications table for therapeutic cytapheresis lists 'polycythemia vera; erythrocytosis' (modifying condition: polycythemia vera) as Category I, procedure erythrocytapheresis, typical course 'Variable'.
source_references.locator: p.1 (AABB Technical Manual, 20th ed., Table 25-6, Indications for Therapeutic Cytapheresis)

source_references.supports: The same passage notes massive thrombocytosis (>1,000,000/µL) can occur in polycythemia vera, and separately lists symptomatic thrombocytosis as ASFA Category II for thrombocytapheresis given daily (QD) with a variable course.
source_references.locator: p.1 (AABB Technical Manual, 20th ed., Table 25-6/surrounding text)

questions.teaching_point: Polycythemia vera/erythrocytosis is an ASFA Category I indication for erythrocytapheresis with a variable, individualized treatment course, distinguishing it from other cytapheresis indications (hyperleukocytosis, thrombocytosis, inflammatory bowel disease) that use different procedures and carry Category II or III designations with defined schedules.

group.topic_path: 5. Apheresis › Specialized Therapeutic Hemapheresis and Phlebotomy › Therapeutic Phlebotomy › Hereditary Hemochromatosis

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with hereditary hemochromatosis (a genetic disorder causing excess iron absorption with accumulation of iron in tissues and organs) is enrolled in a therapeutic phlebotomy program at a blood center. He is in the iron depletion phase, undergoing regular removal of 500 mL of whole blood to lower his serum ferritin toward an acceptable level. The blood center's medical director wants to use these units for allogeneic transfusion without labeling each unit with the patient's diagnosis of hereditary hemochromatosis, and wants to phlebotomize him more frequently than the standard donation interval otherwise permits. Which of the following is required for the blood center to do this?

options.text: Documentation that the AABB Standard 5.6.7.1 allogeneic-donor criteria alone have been met, with no further regulatory action needed
FDA approval of an "exception or alternative procedure" for the facility under 21 CFR 640.120
A physician attestation that the patient has transitioned from the iron depletion phase to the maintenance phase
Laboratory documentation that the patient's serum ferritin has normalized to the reference range
Switching collection from whole blood phlebotomy to red cell collection by apheresis

questions.explanation_correct: Per the AABB Technical Manual, units from therapeutic phlebotomy donors intended for allogeneic use must generally be labeled with the condition requiring phlebotomy (e.g., hereditary hemochromatosis), and standard donation-frequency limits apply. Waiving both the labeling requirement and the frequency limitation requires that the FDA has approved an "exception or alternative procedure" under 21 CFR 640.120 for that facility/program.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. AABB Standard 5.6.7.1 (with 21 CFR 630.15(a)(2)) permits therapeutic phlebotomy units to be used allogeneically when the donor meets standard allogeneic donor criteria, but this alone does not waive the labeling requirement or frequency limitations — that requires the separate FDA exception described in the text.

Correct. The text states that allogeneic donation from individuals such as those with hereditary hemochromatosis is accepted without the diagnosis label and without frequency restriction only "if the FDA has approved an 'exception or alternative procedure' under 21 CFR 640.120," and that "such variances can waive both the labeling requirement and limitations on the frequency of donation."

Incorrect. The passage describes an iron depletion phase (lowering ferritin) followed by a maintenance phase (keeping ferritin low) as the treatment structure, but does not identify treatment phase as the mechanism controlling the labeling or frequency waiver for allogeneic use.

Incorrect. Ferritin normalization is part of the clinical goal of the iron depletion phase but is not described in the text as the regulatory basis for waiving unit labeling or donation frequency limits.

Incorrect. The text notes that RBC collection by apheresis can be considered as an alternative to standard 500 mL whole blood phlebotomy, but this is a collection-method option, not a mechanism for waiving the labeling or frequency requirements for allogeneic use.

source_references.supports: Hemochromatosis is a genetic iron-overload disorder treated by therapeutic phlebotomy (500 mL WB, or RBC apheresis) using an iron-depletion phase then a maintenance phase; units may be used allogeneically per AABB Std 5.6.7.1/21 CFR 630.15(a)(2), generally with disease labeling, unless an FDA 21 CFR 640.120 exception/alternative procedure waives labeling and frequency limits.

group.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
group.topic_path: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events › Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury › Diagnosis and Differential Diagnosis › Consensus Definition

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man receives 2 units of RBCs during elective surgery. One hour after the transfusion is completed, he develops fever, chills, dyspnea, and hypotension. Arterial blood gas on FiO2 0.4 shows a PaO2 of 100 mm Hg (PaO2/FiO2 ratio = 250). Frontal chest radiograph shows new bilateral pulmonary edema, and there is no clinical or laboratory evidence of circulatory overload. TRALI is suspected. TRALI is considered a form of ARDS, and its diagnostic criteria draw on the Berlin definition of ARDS, which grades hypoxemia severity by the PaO2/FiO2 ratio. Based on this patient's PaO2/FiO2 ratio, which hypoxemia severity category applies, and is the overall picture consistent with TRALI?

options.text: Severe ARDS category
Moderate ARDS category, based on a PaO2/FiO2 ratio lower than the mild range
Mild ARDS category (PaO2/FiO2 ratio between 200 and 300 mm Hg), consistent with TRALI
TRALI is excluded, because a PaO2/FiO2 ratio of 250 mm Hg does not meet Berlin oxygenation criteria
Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO), given this degree of hypoxemia

questions.explanation_correct: TRALI is a form of ARDS, and the Berlin definition of ARDS grades hypoxemia severity by the PaO2/FiO2 ratio, with mild disease defined as a ratio between 200 mm Hg and 300 mm Hg. This patient's ratio of 250 mm Hg (PaO2 100 mm Hg / FiO2 0.4) falls within that mild range. Combined with the timing (onset within hours, well within the 6-hour window described for TRALI), new bilateral pulmonary edema on frontal chest radiograph, and absence of evidence of circulatory overload, this presentation fits the consensus picture used to diagnose TRALI as a form of ARDS.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. The source specifies the mild hypoxemia band as a PaO2/FiO2 ratio of 200-300 mm Hg; a ratio of 250 mm Hg falls within this mild range, not a more severe category defined by a lower ratio.
Incorrect. Moderate hypoxemia corresponds to a PaO2/FiO2 ratio below the mild range of 200-300 mm Hg; this patient's ratio of 250 mm Hg falls within the mild band, not below it.

Correct. A PaO2/FiO2 ratio of 250 mm Hg falls within the mild hypoxemia range (200-300 mm Hg) described by the Berlin definition of ARDS, which is used to characterize TRALI; the clinical timing and bilateral pulmonary edema fit the described picture of TRALI.
Incorrect. A ratio of 250 mm Hg falls within the mild hypoxemia range (200-300 mm Hg), so oxygenation criteria for this category of ARDS/TRALI are met, not excluded.

Incorrect. The vignette specifies no evidence of circulatory overload, and the described clinical pattern (fever, chills, dyspnea, hypotension, new bilateral pulmonary edema arising within hours of transfusion) is characteristic of TRALI rather than TACO.

source_references.supports: States TRALI is a form of ARDS; describes the Berlin definition categorizing ARDS by degree of hypoxemia using the PaO2/FiO2 ratio, with mild defined as 200-300 mm Hg; also notes TRALI symptoms (fever, chills, dyspnea, hypotension, hypoxia, new/worsening bilateral pulmonary edema) typically arise within 6 hours of transfusion, most within 1-2 hours.

questions.teaching_point: TRALI's diagnostic framework borrows from the Berlin definition of ARDS: new bilateral pulmonary edema on frontal chest radiograph, onset within hours of transfusion (up to 6 hours, most within 1-2 hours), and hypoxemia graded by PaO2/FiO2 ratio, with mild disease defined as a ratio of 200-300 mm Hg, in the absence of evidence of circulatory overload.

group.topic_path: 10. Surgery Patients › Alternatives to Transfusion: Perioperative Blood Management › Preoperative

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with a history of multiple transfusions has anti-Vel, an antibody to a high-frequency red cell antigen, and is scheduled for elective total hip arthroplasty in 6 weeks. The transfusion service has been unable to locate compatible allogeneic red cell units for her. Her hemoglobin and iron studies are normal, and she has no bleeding disorder. Which of the following is the most appropriate preoperative blood management strategy for this patient?

options.text: Preoperative autologous blood donation (PAD)
Postpone the surgery indefinitely until compatible allogeneic units are identified
Proceed with type and screen only, planning to rely on emergency-release uncrossmatched blood if bleeding occurs
Refer to a presurgical anemia clinic for intravenous iron therapy
Use the maximal surgical blood order schedule (MSBOS) to order 2 units of type and crossmatched blood

questions.explanation_correct: Although presurgical autologous donation (PAD) is generally not indicated for most patients today given the safety of the current allogeneic supply (NAT-tested), the source specifically states PAD remains appropriate when a patient has a rare blood type, has developed alloantibodies to high-frequency antigens, has combinations of multiple antigens requiring very rare phenotype-matched products, or has strongly held religious convictions against allogeneic transfusion. This patient's anti-Vel (a high-frequency antigen antibody) with an inability to locate compatible allogeneic units places her squarely in the indicated-use category for PAD, and the 6-week window before surgery is consistent with the timeframe during which preoperative autologous collection occurs.

option_rationales.text: Correct: alloantibodies to high-frequency antigens are one of the specific scenarios in which PAD remains appropriate, since the patient's own blood may be the only readily compatible source.

Indefinite postponement is not the described strategy; the source instead identifies PAD as the proactive alternative for patients with alloantibodies to high-frequency antigens rather than simply delaying surgery.

Emergency-release uncrossmatched blood is described as the backup plan for unexpected intraoperative bleeding when routine blood is not ready, not as a planned primary strategy for a patient with a known antibody to a high-frequency antigen.

Presurgical anemia clinics and iron therapy address preoperative anemia, which this patient does not have (normal hemoglobin and iron studies); her issue is antibody-related incompatibility, not anemia.

The MSBOS provides institution-derived guidance on typical blood ordering for common procedures based on expected utilization; it does not resolve the specific problem of finding compatible units for a patient alloimmunized to a high-frequency antigen.

source_references.supports: PAD is counterproductive for most patients now except when the patient has a rare blood type, alloantibodies to high-frequency antigens, combinations of multiple antigens requiring rare phenotype-matched products, or strong religious convictions.

Preoperative (autologous) collection occurs during the 5 to 6 weeks immediately preceding a scheduled elective surgical procedure and requires a physician order plus blood bank medical director approval.
For procedures where blood is rarely transfused no preoperative orders are needed; the backup plan for unexpected bleeding is emergency-release uncrossmatched blood.

questions.teaching_point: Presurgical autologous donation (PAD) has largely fallen out of favor given the safety of the modern NAT-tested allogeneic supply and the high wastage rate (14-50%) of collected autologous units, but it remains indicated in specific circumstances: rare blood type, alloantibodies to high-frequency antigens, multi-antigen combinations requiring rare phenotype-matched units, or firmly held patient convictions against allogeneic blood.

group.topic_path: 10. Surgery Patients › Alternatives to Transfusion: Perioperative Blood Management › Intraoperative/Postoperative

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man undergoes an uncomplicated primary total knee arthroplasty. Per hospital protocol, blood draining from a wound drain at the surgical site is collected postoperatively and reinfused through a microaggregate filter without additional processing (i.e., not washed). Approximately 30 minutes after reinfusion, he develops fever and chills. His vital signs are otherwise stable, and a subsequent workup shows no laboratory evidence of hemolysis. Which of the following best explains this reaction?

options.text: Presence of activated coagulation factors, fibrin degradation products, and cytokines in the unwashed salvaged blood
ABO incompatibility from inadvertent administration of the reinfused blood to the wrong patient
Bacterial contamination from reinfusing blood collected more than 6 hours earlier
Reinfusion of a volume of salvaged blood exceeding the recommended 1,400 mL limit
Mechanical hemolysis of red cells caused by the red cell washing/processing step

questions.explanation_correct: Postoperative blood salvage collects blood from a surgical-site drain and reinfuses it, with or without processing, through a microaggregate filter. This blood is characteristically dilute, partially hemolyzed, and defibrinated, and it carries a significant, well-recognized risk of producing febrile (non-hemolytic-type) transfusion reactions because it contains activated coagulation factors, fibrin degradation products, and cytokines generated at the surgical site. This is the specific mechanism the source text attributes to fever after reinfusion of unwashed postoperative salvage blood, consistent with this patient's presentation of fever/chills without evidence of hemolysis.

option_rationales.text: Correct. The source text explicitly identifies activated coagulation factors, fibrin degradation products, and cytokines in postoperative salvage blood as the cause of febrile nonhemolytic-type reactions after reinfusion.

Incorrect. A stated advantage of postoperative salvage is that because the blood never leaves the patient's bedside, there is no risk of it being transfused to the wrong person, eliminating this mechanism.

Incorrect. The source specifies that postoperative salvage blood must be reinfused within 6 hours of collection or discarded, but the vignette does not indicate a delay, and the febrile mechanism described in the text is cytokine/coagulation-factor mediated, not bacterial contamination from a timing violation.

Incorrect. The 1,400 mL figure in the source is the recommended maximum volume to be reinfused, but exceeding it is not the mechanism described for fever/chills, and no specific volume is given in the vignette.

Incorrect. This blood was reinfused 'without additional processing' (unwashed), so a washing-related mechanical hemolysis step does not apply, and the workup in the vignette showed no evidence of hemolysis.

source_references.supports: Postoperative salvage: collected from a surgical-site drain, reinfused with/without processing via a microaggregate filter; blood is dilute, partially hemolyzed, defibrinated; carries significant risk of febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reactions from activated coagulation factors, fibrin degradation products, and cytokines; no wrong-patient risk since blood stays at bedside.
source_references.locator: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.295

source_references.supports: Recommended maximum reinfusion volume of no more than 1,400 mL, and requirement that blood be reinfused within 6 hours of collection or discarded.
source_references.locator: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.295

questions.teaching_point: Postoperative wound-drain blood salvage, especially when reinfused unwashed, carries a specific risk of febrile (nonhemolytic-type) reactions from activated coagulation factors, fibrin degradation products, and cytokines, distinct from wrong-patient/ABO risk (absent because blood never leaves the bedside), the 6-hour discard rule, and the 1,400 mL volume ceiling.

group.topic_path: 10. Surgery Patients › Alternatives to Transfusion: Perioperative Blood Management › Intraoperative/Postoperative › Acute Normovolemic Hemodilution

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with a preoperative hemoglobin of 13.5 g/dL is undergoing a radical prostatectomy anticipated to cause substantial intraoperative blood loss. Immediately before the surgical incision, in the operating room, the anesthesiologist withdraws several units of the patient's own whole blood while simultaneously infusing an isotonic crystalloid solution to keep the patient's intravascular volume constant. The withdrawn units are labeled and held for reinfusion later in the case as blood loss occurs. This technique intentionally lowers the patient's hematocrit before major surgical blood loss begins, so that red cell mass lost during surgery is diluted blood rather than the patient's baseline (higher-hematocrit) blood, and the withdrawn autologous units can later be returned. To maintain normovolemia during this procedure, what volume of crystalloid solution should be infused for each unit (volume) of whole blood withdrawn?

options.text: An equal volume (1:1 ratio) of crystalloid per volume of whole blood withdrawn
Twice the volume (2:1 ratio) of crystalloid per volume of whole blood withdrawn
Three times the volume (3:1 ratio) of crystalloid per volume of whole blood withdrawn
Four times the volume (4:1 ratio) of crystalloid per volume of whole blood withdrawn
One-third the volume (1:3 ratio) of crystalloid per volume of whole blood withdrawn

questions.explanation_correct: This scenario describes acute normovolemic hemodilution (ANH): whole blood is collected in the operating room immediately before surgery while crystalloid or colloid is infused to keep blood volume normal while lowering hematocrit. The source states the replacement ratio is 3:1 for crystalloid (and 1:1 for colloid), so three volumes of crystalloid are given for every one volume of whole blood withdrawn.

option_rationales.text: A 1:1 ratio is the stated replacement ratio for colloid, not crystalloid, in ANH; because crystalloid distributes more broadly than colloid and expands the intravascular space less efficiently per volume infused, an equal-volume crystalloid replacement would not adequately maintain intravascular volume.
A 2:1 ratio is not the ratio given in the source for either crystalloid or colloid replacement during ANH.
Correct: the source specifies a 3:1 crystalloid-to-blood replacement ratio for ANH, which maintains normovolemia while intentionally decreasing hematocrit.
A 4:1 ratio overstates the crystalloid volume beyond the 3:1 ratio given in the source for ANH.
A 1:3 ratio is the reciprocal of the correct crystalloid ratio and would provide far too little volume replacement, risking hypovolemia rather than maintaining normovolemia.

source_references.source_id: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.293
source_references.supports: ANH involves collecting whole blood with concurrent infusion of crystalloid or colloid to maintain normal blood volume while decreasing hematocrit; the replacement ratio is 3:1 for crystalloid and 1:1 for colloid; patients should start with hemoglobin of at least 12.0 g/dL; the procedure is performed in the operating room immediately before the surgical procedure begins.

source_references.source_id: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.304
source_references.supports: Summary chart confirms ANH takes place in the operating room, with 1 to 3 units of whole blood collected and the patient's volume replaced with colloid or crystalloid, then reinfused during the surgical procedure.

questions.teaching_point: Acute normovolemic hemodilution is performed in the operating room immediately before surgery: whole blood is withdrawn and replaced with crystalloid at a 3:1 ratio (or colloid at 1:1) to preserve normovolemia while lowering hematocrit, sparing red cell mass for later reinfusion as surgical blood loss occurs.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old patient with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) is scheduled for an elective cholecystectomy under general anesthesia. Her baseline hemoglobin is 8.2 g/dL and she has no history of acute chest syndrome, stroke, or recent vaso-occlusive crisis. The surgical team consults the blood bank regarding preoperative RBC transfusion. According to standard transfusion guidelines for patients with sickle cell disease who require general anesthesia, what is the most appropriate preoperative hemoglobin target for this patient?

options.text: 7 g/dL
8 g/dL
9 g/dL
10 g/dL
12 g/dL

questions.explanation_correct: Published transfusion guidelines for RBC transfusion in patients with sickle cell disease older than 4 months specifically list 'preoperatively when general anesthesia is planned' as an indication for transfusion, with a stated target hemoglobin of 10 g/dL. Preoperative simple transfusion to this target is used to reduce perioperative sickling-related complications when general anesthesia is planned.

option_rationales.text: 7 g/dL is a commonly cited restrictive transfusion trigger used in general critically ill or hospitalized patients, but it is not the target specified for preoperative transfusion in sickle cell disease patients undergoing general anesthesia.
8 g/dL is close to this patient's baseline but is not the hemoglobin target listed in sickle cell disease-specific preoperative transfusion guidance.
9 g/dL is an intermediate value not specified anywhere in the sickle cell disease transfusion guideline table as the preoperative target.
Correct. The transfusion guideline table for sickle cell disease patients lists a target hemoglobin of 10 g/dL specifically for the indication of surgery requiring general anesthesia.
12 g/dL exceeds the stated target and is not supported by the source guideline; overshooting the target is unnecessary and not part of the recommended approach.

source_references.supports: Table 24-4 (Transfusion Guidelines for RBCs in Patients Older than 4 Months) lists sickle cell disease indications for RBC transfusion, including 'preoperatively when general anesthesia is planned (target hemoglobin = 10 g/dL).'

questions.teaching_point: In sickle cell disease, preoperative simple transfusion to a target hemoglobin of approximately 10 g/dL is a recognized, distinct indication for RBC transfusion when general anesthesia is planned, separate from other SCD-specific indications such as stroke, acute chest syndrome, splenic sequestration, aplastic crisis, or recurrent priapism.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman is admitted with severe warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (WAIHA). Her direct antiglobulin test is positive, and her serum reacts with essentially all reagent and autologous red cells tested at the antiglobulin phase, consistent with a panreactive warm autoantibody. Review of her history confirms she has not received any RBC transfusion in the past 120 days. Because warm autoantibodies can mask an underlying, clinically significant alloantibody, the blood bank must determine whether one is present before RBC units are selected for transfusion. Which of the following is the preferred next step to permit alloantibody identification in this patient?

options.text: Perform autologous adsorption of the patient's serum using her own red cells
Perform allogeneic (heterologous) adsorption using group O reagent red cells
Adsorb the serum with rabbit erythrocyte stroma (RESt) before repeating the panel
Test the serum against red cells of a rare Rh phenotype, such as Rhnull
Treat the patient's red cells with dithiothreitol (DTT) to disrupt the autoantibody before retesting

questions.explanation_correct: For patients who have not received a transfusion within the past 120 days, autologous adsorption is the preferred method for removing the autoantibody so that any underlying alloantibody can be identified. Because this patient's own red cells have not been altered by recently transfused donor cells, autologous adsorption reliably strips the autoantibody while retaining any true alloantibody in the adsorbed serum for testing.

option_rationales.text: Correct: the source states that for patients not transfused in the past 120 days, autologous adsorption is the preferred method of removing the autoantibody to permit alloantibody identification.

Incorrect: allogeneic (heterologous) adsorption is reserved for recent transfusion recipients, since circulating donor cells could adsorb any true alloantibody along with the autoantibody during an autologous adsorption in that setting; this patient has not been recently transfused, so autologous adsorption is preferred instead.

Incorrect: RESt possesses I, H, and IH-like structures and is used to adsorb cold-reactive autoantibodies (and can also remove anti-B and anti-P1), unmasking clinically significant warm-reacting antibodies obscured by cold agglutinins — it is not the technique used to resolve a panreactive warm autoantibody like this patient's.

Incorrect: testing against red cells of a rare Rh phenotype (e.g., Rhnull) to determine autoantibody specificity has limited clinical or practical application, and compatible blood of such a rare phenotype is unlikely to be available even if a distinct reaction pattern were found.

Incorrect: dithiothreitol (or 2-mercaptoethanol) is used to disrupt IgM-mediated spontaneous agglutination that interferes with accurate ABO/Rh typing and DAT interpretation, not to identify an alloantibody masked by a warm (IgG-type) autoantibody.

source_references.locator: p.1 (RBC Transfusion in Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia)
source_references.supports: States that for patients not transfused in the past 120 days, autologous adsorption is the preferred method of removing autoantibody to permit alloantibody identification; allogeneic adsorption is used for recent transfusion recipients.

source_references.locator: p.1 (Specificity of Autoantibody / Selection of Blood for Transfusion)
source_references.supports: States that testing against rare-phenotype red cells to determine autoantibody specificity has limited clinical/practical application and that compatible blood of a rare phenotype is unlikely to be available.
Describes use of DTT/2-ME to disrupt IgM warm autoagglutinin causing spontaneous agglutination and typing/DAT interference, distinct from alloantibody identification.

source_references.source_id: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices
source_references.supports: Explains RESt adsorbs cold-reacting autoantibodies (I/H/IH-like) to unmask warm-reacting antibodies, and that autologous RBC adsorption requires no transfusion within the prior interval because donor cells may adsorb alloantibodies.

questions.teaching_point: When a warm autoantibody masks a potential underlying alloantibody, the choice between autologous and allogeneic adsorption depends on recent transfusion history: autologous adsorption is preferred if the patient has not been transfused within the relevant window, while allogeneic adsorption is used for recently transfused patients whose circulating donor cells would confound autologous adsorption.

group.topic_path: 10. Surgery Patients › Transfusion Therapy in Solid Organ Transplantation › Transfusion Therapy › Kidney

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman with end-stage renal disease is undergoing pretransplant evaluation for a living-donor kidney transplant from her sister. Donor and recipient are ABO compatible, and the lymphocyte (cellular) crossmatch is negative. HLA typing shows the donor and recipient share only 2 of 6 typed HLA antigens (a partial mismatch). Which of the following best describes the significance of this HLA mismatch for proceeding with transplantation?

options.text: The transplant should not proceed because an HLA-identical, ABO-compatible donor is an absolute requirement for successful kidney transplantation
The donor should be excluded and the pair entered into a kidney paired donation program to obtain a better HLA match
A desensitization protocol with plasmapheresis and intravenous immune globulin should be initiated before transplantation because of the HLA mismatch
Renal transplants are not routinely HLA matched, and because ABO compatibility and the lymphocyte crossmatch are both favorable, the partial HLA mismatch does not preclude transplantation
ABO compatibility alone determines the outcome of this transplant, so the lymphocyte crossmatch result carries no clinical weight

questions.explanation_correct: ABO and lymphocyte crossmatch compatibility, not HLA matching, are described as the most important factors in determining the immediate outcome of kidney transplantation. Renal transplants are explicitly noted to be, unlike HSCT, not routinely HLA matched. With ABO compatibility present and a negative crossmatch (indicating no preformed donor-reactive antibody), a partial HLA mismatch alone does not preclude proceeding with transplantation.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. HLA-identical, ABO-compatible siblings give the best graft survival rates, but the source states such donors are 'rarely available' and are not described as an absolute requirement — ABO compatibility and a negative crossmatch are sufficient to proceed.

Incorrect. Kidney paired donation is described as a strategy for ABO- or HLA-incompatible potential living donors (e.g., a positive crossmatch or ABO incompatibility); here the donor is ABO compatible with a negative crossmatch, so exchange is not indicated for a mere partial HLA mismatch.

Incorrect. Desensitization protocols (antibody suppression, splenectomy, plasmapheresis, IVIG) are described as facilitating ABO- or crossmatch-incompatible transplantation, not as a routine response to HLA mismatch when ABO compatibility and crossmatch are both favorable.

Correct. The source states ABO and lymphocyte crossmatch compatibility 'remain the most important factors' in immediate kidney transplant outcomes, and that renal transplants, unlike HSCT, 'are not routinely HLA matched.'
Incorrect. The source pairs ABO compatibility together with lymphocyte crossmatch compatibility as the two most important factors — the crossmatch is not dismissed as clinically irrelevant.

source_references.supports: States that ABO and lymphocyte crossmatch compatibility remain the most important factors in determining immediate kidney transplant outcomes, and that unlike HSCT, renal transplants are not routinely HLA matched.
source_references.locator: AABB Technical Manual, 20th ed., p.1 (HLA Testing and Transplantation section)

source_references.supports: Notes that the best graft survival rates occur with HLA-identical, ABO-compatible sibling donors, but that such donors are rarely available, supporting that HLA identity is not an absolute requirement.
source_references.locator: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.509

source_references.supports: Describes kidney paired donation (KPD) as a strategy for patients with an ABO- or HLA-incompatible potential living donor to exchange donors.
source_references.locator: AABB Technical Manual, 20th ed., p.1 (KPD section)

source_references.supports: Describes desensitization protocols (antibody suppression, splenectomy, plasmapheresis, IVIG) as facilitating ABO- or crossmatch-incompatible kidney transplantation.
source_references.locator: AABB Technical Manual, 20th ed., p.1 (Kidney Transplants section)

questions.teaching_point: For renal transplantation, ABO compatibility and a negative lymphocyte crossmatch are the key immediate immunologic determinants of outcome; unlike HSCT, routine HLA matching is not required, though HLA-identical ABO-compatible sibling donors (when available) give the best long-term graft survival.

group.topic_path: 10. Surgery Patients › Transfusion Therapy in Solid Organ Transplantation › Transfusion Therapy › Pancreas

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with type 1 diabetes mellitus and long-standing diabetic nephropathy underwent a deceased-donor kidney transplant 18 months ago. His glycemic control has remained poor despite intensive insulin therapy, and he is now being evaluated by the transplant team for a pancreas transplant to be performed after his prior kidney transplant (i.e., pancreas-after-kidney, PAK). Which of the following statements about this category of pancreas transplantation is most accurate?

options.text: HLA matching has a major effect on graft survival in this transplant category
This category accounts for the majority (81%) of all pancreas transplants performed
This category accounts for only 5% of all pancreas transplants performed
HLA matching has minimal effect on graft survival in this transplant category
Islet cell transplantation, rather than whole-organ pancreas transplantation, is the standard approach for this category

questions.explanation_correct: The reference text states that HLA matching has a major effect on graft survival in pancreas transplantation, particularly in the pancreas-after-kidney (PAK) and pancreas-alone (PA) categories. Since this patient's pancreas transplant is being performed after his prior kidney transplant, he falls into the PAK category, for which HLA matching is specifically noted to be an important determinant of graft survival.

option_rationales.text: Correct. The source specifically states HLA matching has a major effect on graft survival, particularly in pancreas-after-kidney and pancreas-alone transplants, which includes this patient's category.
Incorrect. Simultaneous pancreas-kidney (SPK) transplantation, not pancreas-after-kidney, accounts for the majority (81%) of pancreas transplants performed.
Incorrect. Pancreas-alone transplantation, not pancreas-after-kidney, accounts for 5% of pancreas transplants; pancreas-after-kidney accounts for 12%.
Incorrect. This is the opposite of the source, which states HLA matching has a major effect on graft survival in this category, not a minimal one.

Incorrect. The source indicates islet cell transplantation has been used to restore pancreas function because of increased risk of myocardial complications with pancreas transplantation generally, but it does not state that islet transplantation is the standard approach for the pancreas-after-kidney category specifically.

source_references.supports: Passage states the majority of pancreas transplants are simultaneous pancreas-kidney (81%), followed by pancreas-after-kidney (12%) and pancreas-alone (5%); HLA matching has a major effect on graft survival particularly in pancreas-after-kidney and pancreas-alone transplants; and islet cell transplantation has been used to restore pancreas function because of increased risk of myocardial complications with pancreas transplantation.

questions.teaching_point: Know the three categories of pancreas transplantation (SPK 81%, PAK 12%, PA 5%) and that HLA matching has a particularly significant impact on graft survival in the PAK and PA-alone categories, distinct from SPK.

group.topic_path: 13. Neutrophils › Neutrophil/Granulocyte Transfusions – Primary Clinical Issues

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man is receiving induction chemotherapy for acute myeloid leukemia. On hospital day 10 his absolute neutrophil count is 150/µL. He develops fever to 39.2°C, and blood cultures grow Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Despite 4 days of broad-spectrum antibiotics he remains febrile and bacteremic. His marrow hypoplasia is expected to be reversible, and he otherwise has a reasonable chance for survival. The transfusion service determines he meets criteria for granulocyte transfusion, and a granulocyte pheresis unit is collected from a G-CSF-mobilized donor. Which of the following is required before this component can be released for transfusion, based on its cellular composition?

options.text: ABO crossmatch, because the component contains a significant volume of red blood cells
HLA matching of donor and recipient prior to release
Leukoreduction filtration to remove residual red blood cells before infusion
Refrigerated storage at 1°-6°C until infusion
Confirmation that the recipient's serum is negative for anti-HNA antibodies

questions.explanation_correct: Granulocyte pheresis components contain greater than 1.0 x 10^10 granulocytes along with platelets and 20 to 50 mL of erythrocytes. Because of this significant red cell content, the source text states the granulocyte pheresis unit needs to be crossmatched, i.e., ABO/RBC compatibility testing is required before release, just as it would be for a red cell-containing component.

option_rationales.text: Correct: the passage explicitly states granulocyte pheresis products must be crossmatched because of their significant RBC content.

Incorrect: the source material does not describe HLA matching as a prerequisite for releasing a granulocyte pheresis component; the only compatibility testing it specifies is the RBC-related crossmatch.

Incorrect: leukoreduction removes white blood cells, which would defeat the therapeutic purpose of a component whose entire intent is to deliver a dose of granulocytes; the source does not describe leukoreduction as part of processing these units.
Incorrect: the source states that if granulocytes cannot be transfused immediately they may be stored at 20° to 24°C without agitation, i.e., room temperature, not refrigerated at 1°-6°C.

Incorrect: anti-HNA antibody testing of the recipient is not described in the source as a release requirement for granulocyte transfusion; it is discussed elsewhere in the material in the context of autoimmune neutropenia and TRALI workups, not as a prerequisite for issuing a granulocyte pheresis product.

source_references.source_id: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.361
source_references.supports: Criteria for granulocyte transfusion benefit (fever, ANC <500/µL, septicemia/bacterial infection unresponsive to antibiotics, reversible marrow hypoplasia, reasonable chance for survival); component composition (>1.0x10^10 granulocytes, platelets, 20-50 mL RBCs); requirement that the unit be crossmatched due to significant RBC content; storage at 20-24°C without agitation if not transfused immediately; frequent irradiation given immunocompromised recipients.
