group.topic_path: 16. Hematopoietic Progenitor Cell (HPC) Transplantation › Management of RBC Antigen Incompatibility in Allogeneic Transplantation › Patient Transfusion Management

questions.stem: A 45-year-old patient is 60 days post allogeneic hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) transplantation and has developed mixed chimerism, with both donor-derived and residual recipient RBCs circulating. The patient continues to require RBC transfusion support, but routine serologic phenotyping of the peripheral blood sample is unreliable because it reflects a mixture of two RBC populations. The blood bank obtains a buccal (cheek) swab from the patient and sends it for DNA-based extended RBC genotyping. What is the primary purpose of genotyping the buccal swab sample in this setting?

options.text: To identify the donor's RBC phenotype in place of serologic testing of donor blood
To determine ABO compatibility between donor and recipient prior to proceeding with transplant

To predict the recipient's native (pretransplant) RBC phenotype, since buccal epithelial cells are not replaced by donor hematopoiesis, so antigen-negative units can be selected to limit further alloimmunization
To detect residual host RBCs in the peripheral blood in order to diagnose graft rejection
To resolve a cold-agglutinin-related discrepancy between the forward and reverse ABO grouping

questions.explanation_correct: Buccal (cheek) epithelial cells are not derived from hematopoietic stem cells, so they are not replaced by donor engraftment even when the patient develops mixed chimerism. DNA extracted from a buccal swab therefore reflects the recipient's own native genotype, from which the recipient's native RBC phenotype can be predicted. This lets the blood bank identify alloantigens to avoid and select antigen-negative units for transfusion, ruling out alloantigens even when serologic phenotyping/crossmatching is unreliable, and thereby limiting further alloimmunization in a patient who is already difficult to transfuse.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. The source distinguishes the two sample sources: DNA from the buccal swab (patient's own non-hematopoietic tissue) predicts the recipient's native phenotype, while DNA from the donor (not the patient's buccal cells) is used to predict the donor's phenotype.

Incorrect. The passage describes buccal swab genotyping being used post-transplant in a patient with mixed chimerism to guide transfusion selection, not as a pretransplant ABO compatibility assessment between donor and recipient.

Correct. This directly reflects the source: buccal swab DNA predicts the recipient's native phenotype in mixed chimerism, allowing alloantigens to be ruled out for transfusion and reducing further alloimmunization risk, while also saving time otherwise spent on repeated adsorptions, elutions, and extensive serologic phenotyping.
Incorrect. The source describes buccal genotyping as a tool for predicting native RBC phenotype to guide antigen-matched transfusion, not as a method for diagnosing graft rejection.

Incorrect. Cold-agglutinin-related ABO discrepancy resolution (warming and washing RBCs, cold autoadsorption) is a separate serologic issue discussed elsewhere in the source and is not the reason buccal swabs are used in the mixed-chimerism transplant scenario.

source_references.supports: Buccal swabs are used in HSCT patients with mixed chimerism because DNA from the buccal swab predicts the recipient's native phenotype (while donor DNA predicts donor phenotype), allowing alloantigens to be ruled out for transfusion and reducing further alloimmunization in a difficult-to-transfuse patient.

questions.teaching_point: In allogeneic HPC transplant recipients with mixed chimerism, serologic RBC phenotyping becomes unreliable because both donor and recipient RBCs are present. Buccal swab DNA-based genotyping is a practical solution because buccal epithelial cells remain of recipient origin and are unaffected by hematopoietic engraftment, allowing determination of the recipient's native phenotype to guide antigen-negative RBC selection and limit alloimmunization.

group.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
group.topic_path: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management › Current Good Manufacturing Practice › Recalls and FDA Enforcement Activities

questions.stem: A regional blood collection facility learns that a donor who gave a unit of RBCs three weeks earlier was later found reactive for HBsAg on repeat testing. Facility staff trace the unit and discover it was already transfused to a hospital patient before the collection facility could intervene. The facility notifies the hospital transfusion service under its recall procedures. Which of the following best describes the nature of this recall action?

options.text: The collection facility must retrieve and destroy the physical unit as part of completing the recall
Because the product has already been transfused, the recall functions as a notification that the product failed to meet standards for safety, purity, or potency
The FDA must independently order a mandatory recall in this situation because voluntary recall procedures do not apply once a unit has been transfused
The recall classification (Class I, II, or III) is determined by whether the unit still remains in the facility's inventory at the time of the recall
A health hazard evaluation is not required in this case because the product has already left circulation through transfusion

questions.explanation_correct: Recalls of blood and blood components differ from most drug recalls because the unit may already have been transfused, meaning there can be no physical recall or return of the product. In that circumstance, the recall is effectively a notification to the transfusion service that the product failed to meet standards for safety, purity, or potency, prompting downstream recipient follow-up rather than physical retrieval.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. Once a unit has been transfused it is no longer under the manufacturer's control and cannot be physically retrieved or returned; the source explicitly notes there can be no physical recall of a transfused unit.

Correct. The source states that when blood or a blood component has already been transfused, a recall is really a notification that the product failed to meet standards for safety, purity, and potency.

Incorrect. The FDA rarely exercises its authority to order a recall and instead relies on the voluntary recall policy under 21 CFR Part 7, regardless of whether the unit was already transfused; voluntary recalls can be initiated by the manufacturer or requested by the FDA.

Incorrect. Recall classification as Class I, II, or III is based on a health hazard evaluation of the potential for the product to cause serious health problems, not on whether product remains physically available in inventory.

Incorrect. Recalls are accompanied by a health hazard evaluation performed by FDA medical staff and scientists (21 CFR 7.41) to determine if the product caused or could cause harm to a recipient, independent of whether the unit was already transfused.

source_references.locator: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.585
source_references.supports: Recalls of blood/blood components differ from drug recalls because the unit may already be transfused; in that case the recall functions as notification that the product failed to meet safety, purity, or potency standards. Also supports that voluntary recall policy (21 CFR Part 7) is the FDA's usual mechanism, that recall classification (I/II/III) is based on potential to cause serious health problems, and that recalls include a health hazard evaluation per 21 CFR 7.41.

questions.teaching_point: When a blood component has already been transfused, an FDA recall cannot be a physical retrieval; it functions as a notification that the product failed to meet safety/purity/potency standards. The FDA relies primarily on voluntary recall procedures (21 CFR Part 7), and recall classification (Class I/II/III) is based on a health hazard evaluation of potential harm, not on inventory status.

group.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
group.topic_path: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management › Current Good Manufacturing Practice › Common Violations

questions.stem: An FDA Office of Regulatory Affairs investigator returns to a licensed plasma-manufacturing facility for a follow-up inspection after the facility previously received notice of significant CGMP deficiencies. On this visit, facility management repeatedly denies the investigator access to the key production areas, so the FDA is unable to conduct a meaningful inspection. The manufacturer continues to hold an active biologics license and continues shipping product in interstate commerce. Based specifically on this inability to gain facility access, which enforcement action is the FDA authorized to pursue?

options.text: Issue a Warning Letter
Suspend the manufacturer's license
Revoke the manufacturer's license
Seize the violative product
Refer the matter for criminal prosecution

questions.explanation_correct: According to the source, a manufacturer's license can be revoked for several specific reasons, including "the FDA's inability to gain access to the facility to conduct a meaningful inspection" (in addition to discovery of significant CGMP deficiencies or a determination that the product is unsafe, ineffective, or misbranded, per 21 CFR 601.5). This is a licensed manufacturer, and the scenario describes exactly the access-denial circumstance the text ties to revocation, so revocation is the enforcement action specifically authorized here.

option_rationales.text: A Warning Letter is an advisory action that notifies a manufacturer of a violation and serves as prior notice that the FDA may take further action; the stem already establishes that prior notice of deficiencies occurred, and the source does not tie the Warning Letter specifically to an access-denial event, so it is not the action indicated by these facts.

Suspension applies to licensed manufacturers when reasonable grounds for revocation AND a danger to health exist, giving immediate withdrawal of authorization to ship; the scenario does not establish a danger to health, so suspension is not the best-supported choice compared to revocation, which the source directly links to inspection-access denial.
Correct: the source explicitly lists the FDA's inability to gain facility access for a meaningful inspection as one of the specific grounds for license revocation.

Seizure is a judicial action requiring a court order to condemn and remove violative products already in distribution; it addresses product already in commerce rather than the facility-access problem described.

Prosecution is referral to the Department of Justice for criminal conduct, fraud, or a pattern of violations; the scenario describes an access-denial event during inspection, which the source ties to revocation rather than to criminal referral.

source_references.supports: License revocation grounds explicitly include FDA's inability to gain facility access for a meaningful inspection, plus significant CGMP deficiencies or unsafe/misbranded product determinations.

Summary of Enforcement Actions table distinguishing Warning Letter (advisory, prior notice), Suspension (licensed only, requires danger to health + grounds for revocation, immediate), Revocation (licensed only, includes inability to inspect facility), Seizure (court order, removes violative product), and Prosecution (DOJ referral for criminal conduct/fraud/pattern of violations).

questions.teaching_point: When a licensed blood/plasma manufacturer denies the FDA meaningful inspection access, this is one of the specific statutory grounds for license revocation (21 CFR 601.5), distinct from suspension (requires a danger-to-health element), seizure (judicial removal of already-distributed violative product), and prosecution (criminal referral).

group.domain: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management
group.topic_path: 17. Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine-Specific Administration and Laboratory Management › Hospital Transfusion Services, Transfusion Committee, and Quality Assurance › Regulatory and Accreditation Requirements

questions.stem: The laboratory director of a hospital-based transfusion service is completing the facility's Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA) certification renewal. The facility performs high-complexity testing (ABO/Rh typing, antibody screening, and infectious disease testing) and holds current, unsuspended accreditation from AABB. No separate on-site inspection by the state health department has been scheduled or performed for this cycle. Which of the following best explains why the AABB accreditation is sufficient to satisfy the facility's CLIA certification requirement for this testing?

options.text: CMS has approved AABB as a laboratory accreditation organization with deeming authority, so a successful AABB inspection is accepted by CMS as meeting CLIA requirements through the certificate-of-accreditation pathway
AABB Standards for Blood Banks and Transfusion Services carry the force of law and therefore supersede CLIA regulations for immunohematology testing performed by the facility
AABB accreditation satisfies the CLIA quality system requirements and exempts the facility from having to enroll in an approved proficiency testing program for its regulated analytes
AABB accreditation automatically confers CMS-exempt status, the licensure pathway otherwise limited to nonwaived laboratories in New York and Washington
AABB accreditation supersedes the state's professional licensure requirements for the facility's medical and laboratory personnel

questions.explanation_correct: CMS regulates laboratory testing through CLIA and has approved six laboratory accreditation organizations (AABB, American Osteopathic Association, ASHI, CAP, COLA, and The Joint Commission) whose standards and inspection processes meet CMS requirements. CMS accepts inspections from these approved organizations in place of a direct state or CMS survey; this 'certificate of accreditation' is one of the three recognized pathways to obtaining a CLIA certificate (the others being certificate of compliance via state health department inspection, and CMS-exempt status limited to New York/Washington licensure programs). This is an example of a regulatory agency granting deeming authority to a select accreditation organization.

option_rationales.text: Correct: this is precisely the CLIA certificate-of-accreditation pathway, in which CMS accepts a CMS-approved organization's (e.g., AABB's) inspection as satisfying the CLIA requirement.

Incorrect: the passage explicitly distinguishes regulation from accreditation — regulations have the force of law, while accreditation standards (such as AABB's) are not legally binding and do not supersede CLIA regulation.

Incorrect: CMS requires successful proficiency testing (PT) for ongoing certification of nonwaived testing on regulated analytes regardless of which CLIA pathway is used; accreditation does not exempt a facility from PT enrollment.

Incorrect: CMS-exempt status is a separate, third CLIA pathway specific to state licensure programs for nonwaived laboratories in New York and Washington — it is not something conferred by AABB accreditation.

Incorrect: facilities must separately comply with state professional licensure requirements for medical and laboratory personnel; accreditation by AABB does not replace or supersede these state-level licensure requirements.

source_references.supports: States that regulations have the force of law while accreditation standards are not legally binding, and that CMS/FDA provide federal oversight while some regulatory agencies grant deeming authority to select accreditation organizations.

Describes the three CLIA certification pathways (certificate of compliance, certificate of accreditation, CMS-exempt status), lists the six CMS-approved laboratory accreditation organizations including AABB, and states CMS requires successful proficiency testing for ongoing certification of nonwaived testing.

questions.teaching_point: Regulation (CLIA/CMS, FDA) carries the force of law; accreditation (AABB, CAP, Joint Commission, etc.) is voluntary but CMS grants deeming authority to approved accrediting organizations so their inspections can substitute for direct CLIA survey via the certificate-of-accreditation pathway. Proficiency testing and state licensure requirements remain independently required regardless of accreditation status.

questions.stem: The medical director of a hospital-based transfusion service is preparing a staff education session on regulatory oversight in advance of an upcoming inspection. A resident asks about the scope and legal basis of the Food and Drug Administration's (FDA) authority over blood banking activities. Which of the following statements correctly describes FDA regulation of blood and blood components?

options.text: FDA regulatory authority is limited to donor collection procedures and does not extend to blood collection supplies and instruments, blood establishment computer software, or blood bank reagents
A biologics license may be approved based solely on demonstration that the final product meets specifications, without any requirement to evaluate the manufacturing facility or process

The FDA regulates human blood and blood components as biological products under authority of the Public Health Service Act and the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act, and requires blood establishments to adhere to current good manufacturing practice (CGMP) requirements set out in Title 21 of the Code of Federal Regulations
The statutory basis for FDA regulation of biologics originated with the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act of 1938, enacted after children died from diphtheria antitoxin contaminated with tetanus

FDA authority over blood establishments derives solely from the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act; the Public Health Service Act does not apply to biological products such as blood and blood components

questions.explanation_correct: As a federal agency responsible for food, drugs, medical devices, biologics, veterinary medicine, and tobacco, the FDA promulgates and enforces regulations ensuring the safety and efficacy of biological products, including human blood and blood components, blood collection supplies and instruments, blood establishment computer software (BECS), blood bank reagents, and tests for relevant transfusion-transmitted infections. These regulations are promulgated under both the Public Health Service (PHS) Act and the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic (FD&C) Act and are codified in Parts 210 to 211, 600 to 680, and 800 to 820 of Title 21 of the Code of Federal Regulations, including adherence to current good manufacturing practice (CGMP) requirements, licensing of biological products, and establishment registration.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. The source explicitly states FDA's purview includes blood collection supplies and instruments, blood establishment computer software (BECS), and blood bank reagents in addition to donor screening and collection, not donor collection procedures alone.

Incorrect. Under the PHS Act, a biologics license application can be approved only after the manufacturer demonstrates that both the product and the manufacturing (facility/process) meet applicable requirements — facility evaluation is required, not optional.

Correct. This matches the source description of FDA's dual statutory basis (PHS Act and FD&C Act), its regulatory scope over blood and blood components as biological products, the relevant CFR parts, and the CGMP requirement.

Incorrect on the statute and its origin. The source attributes the diphtheria antitoxin/tetanus contamination deaths of 13 children to passage of the Biologics Control Act of 1902 (Virus-Toxin Law), which was later incorporated into the PHS Act in 1944 as Section 351 — not the FD&C Act of 1938.
Incorrect. The source states regulations for biological products are promulgated under both the PHS Act and the FD&C Act together, not the FD&C Act alone.

source_references.locator: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.575
source_references.supports: FDA regulates human blood and blood components, collection supplies/instruments, BECS, and reagents as biological products under the PHS Act and FD&C Act, codified in 21 CFR Parts 210-211, 600-680, 800-820, including CGMP, licensing, and establishment registration requirements.

source_references.locator: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.575
source_references.supports: History: Biologics Control Act of 1902 (following diphtheria antitoxin/tetanus contamination deaths of 13 children) was incorporated into the PHS Act in 1944 as Section 351, which requires a biologics license before interstate commerce, approvable only after the manufacturer demonstrates the product and manufacturing meet requirements.

questions.teaching_point: FDA regulates blood and blood components as biological products under the joint authority of the PHS Act and FD&C Act (21 CFR Parts 210-211, 600-680, 800-820), covering not just donor collection but also supplies, instruments, BECS software, reagents, and RTTI testing, with CGMP adherence and licensing (including manufacturing facility evaluation) as core requirements. This traces historically to the Biologics Control Act of 1902, later folded into the PHS Act (Section 351) in 1944.

questions.stem: A 450-bed hospital is expanding its Patient Blood Management (PBM) program and hires a full-time Transfusion Safety Officer (TSO) with a nursing background. As part of routine PBM duties, the TSO performs a transfusion record audit, tracks blood utilization metrics, and identifies several physicians with non-compliant transfusion ordering patterns. The TSO then prepares a summary report of these findings for presentation to hospital leadership and staff.

options.text: The TSO coordinates the hospital's transfusion committee and presents the audit and blood-utilization findings to it as part of an active role in educating noncompliant physicians toward evidence-based practice
The TSO functions entirely independently of the transfusion committee, since blood utilization audit and review fall exclusively under the blood bank medical director's authority
The TSO's responsibilities are restricted to bedside nursing education and do not extend to preparing or presenting reports to hospital committees or physicians
The TSO reports audit findings only to the hospital's medical executive committee, and this reporting pathway bypasses the transfusion committee entirely
The TSO's role is limited to managing transfusion reactions and safe blood administration training, with no involvement in committee-level reporting

questions.explanation_correct: The source describes the TSO as coordinating the transfusion committee and providing expertise for it and other hospital committees, and as being capable of preparing and presenting educational materials or reports (including audit and blood utilization findings) to varied audiences, explicitly including the facility's transfusion committee. The audit process is tied directly to physician education: identifying noncompliant physicians and driving adherence to transfusion guidelines through that education.

option_rationales.text: Correct: matches the source's description of the TSO coordinating the transfusion committee and using audit/utilization findings to educate noncompliant physicians and promote guideline adherence.

Incorrect: the source states the TSO coordinates and/or participates in most PBM functions, including audit and blood utilization review, and coordinates the transfusion committee itself — this is not described as the medical director's exclusive domain.

Incorrect: the source explicitly lists the facility's transfusion committee, physicians, laboratorians, and the medical executive committee as audiences for the TSO's educational materials and reports, well beyond bedside nursing education alone.
Incorrect: the source specifically names the facility's transfusion committee as one of the audiences for TSO reports; it is not bypassed in favor of the medical executive committee alone.

Incorrect: while the TSO must be well versed in transfusion reactions and safe blood administration, the source describes a much broader role encompassing audit, metric tracking, education, and committee coordination.

source_references.supports: TSO coordinates and/or participates in PBM functions including transfusion record audit, blood utilization review, and metric tracking; prepares/presents reports to audiences including the facility's transfusion committee; coordinates the transfusion committee and provides expertise for other hospital committees; audit-identified noncompliant physicians are educated to promote guideline adherence.

questions.teaching_point: The Transfusion Safety Officer functions as the operational link between blood utilization/audit data and the hospital transfusion committee, coordinating committee activities and translating audit findings into targeted physician education to drive adherence to transfusion guidelines.

questions.stem: A 46-year-old man undergoes a craniotomy at Hospital A, during which a skull bone flap is removed and sent to the hospital's tissue bank (operated as part of the transfusion service) for autologous storage, with a plan for re-implantation once the patient recovers. Before the planned re-implantation date, the patient is transferred to Hospital B for ongoing rehabilitation, and the stored bone flap is shipped to Hospital B's tissue bank for the re-implantation procedure to be performed there.

options.text: No FDA registration is required at either facility because autologous tissue is always exempt from FDA tissue-establishment registration
Registration is required only if the bone flap was stored for longer than 24 hours before re-implantation
Hospital A's tissue bank becomes required to register with the FDA as a tissue-manufacturing establishment because the tissue was transferred to a different facility before re-implantation
Registration is required only because more than one type of autologous tissue was stored by the same tissue bank
Registration is required only if the surgeon performing the re-implantation is different from the surgeon who recovered the bone flap

questions.explanation_correct: A hospital tissue bank handling autologous tissue is exempt from FDA registration as a tissue-manufacturing establishment only as long as the tissue is retained and re-implanted at the same facility where it was recovered. Once autologous tissue is transferred to a different facility (as occurs here when the patient moves to Hospital B), the originating hospital tissue bank loses its exemption and is required to register with the FDA as a tissue-manufacturing establishment and becomes subject to FDA inspection.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect: the exemption is conditional, not absolute — it applies only when the tissue stays at the same facility until re-implantation; transfer to another facility removes the exemption.
Incorrect: the source describes the registration trigger as transfer of the tissue to another facility, not a specific storage duration threshold.

Correct: per the source, if autologous tissue is transferred to any other facility (e.g., because the patient was transferred), the hospital tissue bank must register with the FDA as a tissue-manufacturing establishment and is subject to FDA inspection.
Incorrect: the number or variety of autologous tissue types stored is not described as a factor affecting FDA registration status.
Incorrect: whether the recovering and re-implanting surgeons are the same individual is not identified in the source as relevant to the FDA registration requirement.

source_references.supports: States that autologous tissue used at the same institution without shipment elsewhere is exempt from FDA tissue-establishment registration, but if transferred to another facility (e.g., patient transfer), the hospital tissue bank must register with the FDA as a tissue-manufacturing establishment and is subject to FDA inspection.

questions.teaching_point: Hospital tissue banks handling autologous tissue are exempt from FDA tissue-establishment registration only if the tissue is retained and re-implanted at the same facility; transferring the tissue to another facility (e.g., due to patient transfer) triggers a requirement to register with the FDA as a tissue-manufacturing establishment and subjects the facility to FDA inspection.

questions.stem: A pretransfusion antibody identification workup is performed on a hospitalized patient's serum using a standard 11-cell panel. The reaction pattern fails to exclude anti-E, anti-Kpa, anti-Jsa, anti-Fya, and anti-Jkb, so no single antibody specificity can yet be confirmed. The autocontrol is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to resolve the antibody identification?

options.text: Elute antibody from the patient's red cells to identify IgG coating the cell surface
Retest the patient's serum against an enzyme-treated panel to separate a specific antibody pair
Test the serum against additional selected cells from another panel, choosing cells whose antigen profiles have minimal overlap among the antibodies still not excluded
Perform warm autoadsorption using the patient's own red cells to remove autoantibody from the serum
Repeat the panel using prewarmed reagents and the prewarm technique at 37°C

questions.explanation_correct: When an initial antibody panel leaves multiple specificities unexcluded, the source text describes testing a selected cell panel as 'perhaps the simplest step to take.' Cells are deliberately chosen from another panel so their antigen profiles have minimal overlap among the antibodies still in question, allowing each additional cell's reaction (positive or negative) to eliminate specificities efficiently. The text's own example panel in which anti-E, anti-Kpa, anti-Jsa, anti-Fya, and anti-Jkb are not excluded is resolved this way, with individual selected cells sequentially eliminating anti-Fya and anti-E while confirming anti-Jkb.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. Elution is used to remove and identify IgG antibody that is coating a patient's red blood cells (e.g., in a positive DAT workup), not to resolve overlapping specificities on a serum panel with a negative autocontrol.

Incorrect. Enzyme-treated panels are a targeted technique for separating specific antibody pairs (such as those affected by enzyme-sensitive versus enzyme-resistant antigens), not the first, simplest step described for resolving multiple unexcluded specificities.

Correct. Testing additional cells selected for minimal antigen overlap among the antibodies in question is described as the simplest next step for resolving an antibody panel that does not yield a clear-cut specificity.

Incorrect. Warm autoadsorption using autologous cells removes autoantibody from serum and is used when an autoantibody is suspected; it is not the indicated technique here, and this scenario does not suggest an autoantibody.
Incorrect. The prewarm technique is used to circumvent reactivity caused by a cold-reactive antibody; there is no indication in this scenario of cold-reactive interference.

source_references.supports: States selected cell panel testing (cells with minimal antigen overlap) is the simplest step when a panel leaves multiple specificities (including anti-E, anti-Kpa, anti-Jsa, anti-Fya, anti-Jkb) unexcluded.
Describes enzyme-treated panels being used to separate specific antibody pairs during identification.
Lists elution as a method to remove IgG antibodies coating a patient's red blood cells.
Lists warm autoadsorption using autologous cells among techniques to address autoantibody in serum.
States prewarming of the test system may be indicated to circumvent reactivity from a cold-reactive antibody.

questions.teaching_point: When an antibody identification panel leaves multiple specificities unexcluded, the simplest and most appropriate first additional technique is to test a selected cell panel chosen for minimal antigen overlap among the remaining candidate antibodies, rather than immediately reaching for problem-specific techniques (enzyme panels for antigen pairs, elution for RBC-bound IgG, autoadsorption for autoantibody, or prewarming for cold-reactive antibody) that address different underlying issues.

questions.stem: During pretransfusion testing, a 28-year-old patient's RBCs are phenotyped and react as Le(a+b−). No history of recent transfusion or pregnancy is noted. Based on the known molecular relationship between the Lewis (Le) and secretor (Se, FUT2) genes, which statement best explains this patient's most likely ABH secretor status and the molecular basis for the RBC phenotype observed?

options.text: Secretor status cannot be inferred from the RBC Lewis phenotype; saliva testing would be required regardless of the Le(a+b−) result

Nonsecretor: without a functional Se (FUT2) fucosyltransferase, type 1 precursor chains are not converted to type 1H, so the Lewis transferase acts on unmodified type 1 chains to form Lea only, and Leb cannot be produced
Secretor: an amorphic Lewis (le) allele blocks Leb synthesis even though the Se gene is functional and actively converting type 1 chains to type 1H
Nonsecretor: absence of a functional H (FUT1) gene enzyme prevents fucosylation of type 2 chains, which is required before Leb can be formed
Secretor: a functional Se (FUT2) enzyme is present and converts type 1 chains to type 1H, which explains why both Lea and Leb would be detectable in this patient's saliva

questions.explanation_correct: Lewis antigen expression depends on the interaction of the Le and Se genes acting on type 1 precursor chains. The Lewis-encoded fucosyltransferase adds fucose to type 1 chains to form Lea. Separately, the Se (FUT2) gene product preferentially acts on type 1 chains in secretory tissue, converting them to type 1H; the Lewis transferase can then act on type 1H to form Leb. In nonsecretors (sese), the Se enzyme is absent, so type 1 chains are never converted to type 1H — Lea is formed and persists, but Leb cannot be made. This is exactly the pattern described for Le(a+b−) individuals, who are uniformly ABH nonsecretors and secrete Lea only, in contrast to Le(a−b+) individuals, who are secretors and produce both Lea and Leb (with little free Lea remaining because it is largely converted to Leb).

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. The source material explicitly maps RBC Lewis phenotype to secretor status (Le(a+b−) = nonsecretor; Le(a−b+) = secretor), so the RBC phenotype is informative, not indeterminate.
Correct. This matches the described mechanism: absent Se (FUT2) activity leaves type 1 chains unconverted to type 1H, so only Lea forms and Le(a+b−)/nonsecretor results.

Incorrect and internally inconsistent — a functional Lewis gene (not an amorphic le allele) is required to make Lea in the first place; an amorphic le allele instead produces the Le(a−b−) phenotype, and this option also misassigns secretor status.

Incorrect mechanism. The H (FUT1) enzyme acts primarily on type 2 chains on the RBC membrane, not on the type 1 secretory chains relevant to Lewis/Leb formation; FUT1 status is not what defines secretor phenotype for Leb synthesis.
Incorrect. This describes the Le(a−b+)/secretor pattern, which is the opposite of the Le(a+b−) phenotype given in the stem.

source_references.supports: States that all Le(a−b+) individuals are ABH secretors and secrete Lea and Leb, while all Le(a+b−) individuals are ABH nonsecretors and secrete only Lea; Table 8-9 maps Le/Se genotype combinations to secreted antigens and RBC phenotype.

Explains that the Se (FUT2) enzyme preferentially acts on type 1 chains in secretory tissue while the H (FUT1) enzyme acts primarily on type 2 chains prevalent on the RBC membrane, underpinning why Se gene function governs secretory-tissue conversion of type 1 chains.
Describes that as secretor (Se) transferase activity converts type 1 chains to type 1H, Leb becomes detectable, confirming that functional Se/FUT2 activity is the prerequisite step for Leb formation.

questions.teaching_point: ABH secretor status can be inferred from RBC Lewis phenotype: Le(a-b+) individuals are secretors (Se/FUT2 functional, converting type 1 chains to type 1H so Lewis transferase can form Leb), while Le(a+b-) individuals are nonsecretors (no FUT2 activity, so only Lea forms). The H (FUT1) gene governs type 2 chain fucosylation on RBCs and is a distinct system from the Se (FUT2) gene, which governs type 1 chain conversion in secretory tissue.

questions.stem: A red cell reference laboratory investigates an unusual Rh phenotype in a transfusion candidate of African ancestry. Molecular genotyping shows the patient carries an RHD*DIIIa-CE(4-7)-D hybrid allele — a gene located within the RHD locus — inherited in cis with an RHCE*ceS allele (which itself encodes a partial e antigen and a V–VS+ phenotype). This combination is recognized as the (C)ceS type 1 haplotype, and the patient's red cells lack the high-prevalence antigen HrB. Which of the following best describes what the RHD*DIIIa-CE(4-7)-D hybrid allele itself encodes?

options.text: A full-strength D antigen combined with a weakened C antigen
The CW antigen (Gln41Arg), the alteration typically described on altered RHCE alleles from individuals of European ancestry
A partial C antigen on a hybrid genetic background that does not itself encode a D antigen, despite residing within the RHD gene
An altered E/e antigen resulting from a Pro226Ala substitution on the fourth extracellular loop of RhCE
The JAHK antigen (p.Ser122Leu), described as the most common cause of partial C expression in individuals of African ancestry

questions.explanation_correct: Per the AABB Technical Manual, RHD*DIIIa-CE(4-7)-D (and the less common RHD-CE(4-7)-D) hybrid alleles are located within the RHD gene but do not encode the D antigen; instead they encode C reactivity on a hybrid genetic background that differs from the normal background. This hybrid, inherited in cis with RHCE*ceS (which encodes partial e and a V–VS+ phenotype), produces the (C)ceS type 1 (r′S) haplotype. Red cells carrying this haplotype express a partial C antigen and lack the high-prevalence antigen HrB — exactly the findings in this patient — which is why partial C expression 'most often results from the inheritance of an RHD*DIIIa-CE(4-7)-D hybrid' rather than from the other mechanisms listed.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. The source explicitly states these hybrid alleles 'are located in the RHD gene but do not encode the D antigen; rather, they encode a C reactivity on a hybrid background.' They do not confer a full-strength D antigen.

Incorrect. CW (Gln41Arg), like CX (Ala36Thr), is the altered-C mechanism described in persons of European ancestry via amino acid changes on the first extracellular loop of RhCe — a different mechanism from the RHD-RHCE hybrid allele described in this African-ancestry patient.

Correct. This matches the source directly: the hybrid allele is intragenic to RHD but encodes C reactivity on a hybrid background rather than D, and pairs with RHCE*ceS to form the (C)ceS type 1/r′S haplotype with partial C and HrB-negative red cells.

Incorrect. Pro226Ala on loop 4 is the amino acid substitution that distinguishes E from e (per Harmening); it is unrelated to the RHD*DIIIa-CE(4-7)-D hybrid mechanism, which affects C antigen expression, not E/e.

Incorrect. JAHK (p.Ser122Leu) and JAL (p.Arg114Trp) are novel antigens associated with altered/partial C, but the source specifies that partial C expression 'most often results from' the RHD*DIIIa-CE(4-7)-D hybrid, not from JAHK.

source_references.supports: RHD*DIIIa-CE(4-7)-D hybrid alleles reside in RHD but do not encode D; they encode C reactivity on a hybrid background and, linked with RHCE*ceS, form the (C)ceS type 1/r'S haplotype; red cells express partial C and lack HrB.
CW (Gln41Arg) and CX (Ala36Thr) are altered-C antigens described in persons of European ancestry, distinct from the African-ancestry hybrid mechanism.
JAHK (p.Ser122Leu) and JAL (p.Arg114Trp) are associated with altered/partial C but are not stated to be the most common cause; the hybrid allele is.

source_references.source_id: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices
source_references.supports: E/e antigens differ by a Pro226Ala substitution on loop 4 of RhCE.

questions.teaching_point: In individuals of African ancestry, partial C antigen expression most often arises from an RHD*DIIIa-CE(4-7)-D hybrid allele that, despite sitting within the RHD locus, does not encode D but instead confers C reactivity on a hybrid background; when inherited in cis with RHCE*ceS it forms the (C)ceS type 1/r'S haplotype, characterized by partial C and loss of the high-prevalence antigen HrB. This is mechanistically distinct from the CW/CX alterations common in European-ancestry RHCE alleles and from the E/e-defining Pro226Ala polymorphism.

group.topic_path: 3. RBCs and RBC Components › Rh and LW Blood Group Systems › Molecular Basis for Rh Antigen Expression › RH Genotyping

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with sickle cell disease is enrolled in a chronic RBC transfusion program and has received several transfusions over the past 3 months. The blood bank wants to determine her complete Rh phenotype (D, C/c, E/e) to select antigen-matched units going forward. Serologic phenotyping performed on her current peripheral blood sample shows a mixed-field agglutination pattern that cannot be reliably interpreted, because the sample contains a mixture of her own red cells and previously transfused donor red cells. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to accurately determine this patient's Rh antigen profile?

options.text: Repeat serologic phenotyping using a different panel of anti-sera reagents
Perform RH genotyping using DNA extracted from a peripheral blood sample
Delay testing for 3 months to allow transfused donor cells to clear before repeating serologic phenotyping
Perform an eluate to differentiate the patient's autologous cells from donor cells serologically
Infer the patient's Rh phenotype from the recorded phenotypes of her most recent donor units

questions.explanation_correct: In patients receiving chronic or massive transfusions, circulating donor red cells make agglutination-based phenotyping unreliable. RH genotyping overcomes this limitation because blood grouping can be determined directly from genomic DNA prepared from a blood sample, and this remains accurate even when the sample was collected after transfusion, since the patient's own genotype does not change regardless of which donor red cells are circulating.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. Switching anti-sera does not resolve the underlying problem: the sample still contains a mixture of patient and donor red cells, so any agglutination-based method remains inaccurate.
Correct. Genotyping is performed on DNA, not on the red cell membrane, so it reliably predicts the patient's own phenotype even when donor cells are present in the circulation.

Incorrect. A patient on a chronic transfusion program continues to receive new transfused cells, so waiting for complete clearance is impractical and unnecessary given that genotyping already overcomes this limitation.

Incorrect. Eluate testing is used to recover antibody from sensitized red cells for antibody identification, not to resolve mixed-field phenotyping results in a chronically transfused patient; this technique is not described as an approach for this problem in the source material.
Incorrect. Donor units are from genetically unrelated individuals; their phenotype has no bearing on the patient's own genetically determined Rh phenotype.

source_references.supports: States that in patients receiving chronic or massive transfusions, donor red cells in the peripheral blood make phenotyping by agglutination inaccurate, and that genotyping overcomes this because blood grouping can be determined from DNA even if the sample was collected after transfusion.

Notes that when a patient has had multiple transfusions and it may not be possible to distinguish the patient's own red cells from transfused red cells, genotyping patient DNA can reliably predict the patient's red cell phenotype.

questions.teaching_point: RH genotyping is especially valuable for typing chronically or massively transfused patients (e.g., sickle cell disease) because it interrogates the patient's genomic DNA rather than circulating red cells, remaining accurate even in the presence of mixed patient/donor red cell populations that confound serologic agglutination testing.

questions.stem: A pregnant woman in her second trimester has a positive antibody screen due to anti-D. Serologic testing shows the father of the baby is D+. To assess whether the fetus is at obligate risk versus partial risk for hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn, molecular RHD zygosity testing is performed on the father's sample. The result shows he carries two functional RHD alleles (homozygous). Which of the following best describes the correct interpretation of this result for management of the current pregnancy?

options.text: All of the father's offspring will inherit a functional RHD allele and be D+, so this pregnancy is obligately at risk and requires ongoing monitoring for HDFN
The fetus has only a 50% chance of inheriting a functional RHD allele, so monitoring may reasonably be deferred pending further testing
A single molecular assay is always sufficient to establish RHD zygosity in this case, since only one genetic mechanism produces a D-negative phenotype
This result means immune-modulating agents such as Rh immune globulin can be safely withheld for the remainder of the pregnancy
Because the father already typed D+ serologically, molecular zygosity testing adds no clinically useful information beyond the serologic result

questions.explanation_correct: Per the source, if the father is homozygous (carries two functional RHD alleles), all of his children will be D+, making this pregnancy obligately at risk for HDFN and requiring monitoring throughout gestation, in contrast to a heterozygous father where the fetus has only a 50% chance of being at risk.

option_rationales.text: Correct: the source states that a homozygous father means all offspring will be D+, so the pregnancy needs to be monitored.
Incorrect: a 50% risk of being D+ describes the heterozygous paternal result, not the homozygous result given in this vignette.

Incorrect: the source notes several different genetic events cause a D-negative phenotype, so multiple assays are often needed to accurately determine RHD zygosity, especially in non-European ethnic groups.

Incorrect: the source indicates fetal/paternal D typing helps avoid unnecessary monitoring and immune-modulating agent use only when the fetus is predicted D-negative; a homozygous father guarantees a D+ fetus, so withholding intervention is not supported.

Incorrect: the source presents molecular RHD zygosity testing specifically because it distinguishes obligate risk (homozygous) from 50% risk (heterozygous) in a way serologic D typing of the father alone cannot.

source_references.supports: Describes paternal RHD zygosity testing for HDFN risk assessment: homozygous father means all children D+ (pregnancy needs monitoring), heterozygous father means 50% fetal risk; multiple genetic events cause D-negative phenotype requiring multiple assays, especially in non-European groups; fetal D typing can prevent unnecessary monitoring and immune-modulating agent use.

questions.teaching_point: Molecular RHD zygosity testing of the father in a pregnancy complicated by maternal anti-D distinguishes obligate fetal risk (paternal homozygosity, all offspring D+) from partial risk (paternal heterozygosity, 50% chance of D+ fetus), guiding whether intensive monitoring or further fetal RHD typing is warranted; multiple assays are often needed since several distinct genetic events can produce a D-negative phenotype, particularly in non-European populations.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old multiply transfused patient's antibody screen is positive with all reagent and panel RBCs except autologous cells, consistent with an antibody to a high-prevalence antigen. To help characterize the antibody, the reference laboratory treats panel RBCs with dithiothreitol (DTT). After DTT treatment, the patient's antibody no longer reacts with the panel cells, yet the same DTT-treated cells continue to express the Kx antigen normally. Which of the following best explains the structural relationship between the Kell glycoprotein and the Xk protein that accounts for this differential effect of DTT?

options.text: The Kell glycoprotein is a single-pass membrane protein whose large extracellular C-terminal domain is heavily folded by disulfide linkages and is covalently linked to a separate Xk protein; reducing agents disrupt the disulfide-dependent Kell epitopes while leaving the physically distinct Xk protein, which carries Kx, intact
The Kell glycoprotein and the Xk protein are the same polypeptide chain, so any agent that denatures Kell antigens should denature Kx antigen equally
Kell antigen expression does not depend on disulfide bonding, so thiol-reducing agents such as DTT have no effect on Kell antigen reactivity
The Kell glycoprotein spans the RBC membrane multiple times, similar to a multi-pass transport protein, which is why it is resistant to proteolytic denaturation
The Kx antigen resides on the same disulfide-folded ectodomain loop of the Kell glycoprotein as the K and k antigens, rather than on a separate protein

questions.explanation_correct: The Kell glycoprotein is a 731-amino-acid, single-pass membrane protein with an intracellular N-terminus and a large extracellular C-terminal domain that is highly folded by disulfide linkages; it is covalently linked to a separate protein, the Xk protein. Thiol-reducing agents such as DTT, 2-mercaptoethanol, AET, and ZZAP destroy Kell antigens (which depend on the disulfide-stabilized conformation of the Kell glycoprotein) but do not destroy Kx, because Kx resides on the structurally distinct Xk protein. This is exactly the pattern described: DTT abolishes reactivity of a Kell-system antibody while Kx antigen expression is preserved.

option_rationales.text: Correct. Matches the described structure: single-pass Kell glycoprotein with a disulfide-folded ectodomain, covalently linked to a separate Xk protein; thiol reduction selectively destroys Kell epitopes while sparing Kx.

Incorrect. The source describes the Kell glycoprotein as covalently linked to 'another protein' (the Xk protein), i.e., two distinct polypeptides, not one shared chain — which is why DTT can selectively abolish Kell reactivity while Kx is preserved.
Incorrect. The source explicitly states that thiol-reducing agents (DTT, 2-ME, AET, and ZZAP) destroy Kell antigens, indicating Kell epitope integrity does depend on disulfide bonding.

Incorrect. The source states the Kell glycoprotein spans the RBC membrane only once (single-pass), not multiple times, and its resistance to ficin/papain (but not trypsin/chymotrypsin) is a separate enzymatic property unrelated to the DTT/Kx distinction being tested here.

Incorrect. Kx is associated with the separate Xk protein, not with the same disulfide-dependent domain of the Kell glycoprotein that carries K/k antigens; this is precisely why Kx survives thiol treatment that destroys Kell antigens.

source_references.supports: Kell glycoprotein is a 731-amino-acid, single-pass RBC membrane protein; N-terminal domain intracellular, large external C-terminal domain highly folded by disulfide linkages; covalently linked with another protein.

The associated Xk protein is found in erythroid and other tissues; Kell antigens are not denatured by ficin/papain but are destroyed by trypsin plus chymotrypsin; thiol-reducing agents (DTT, 2-ME, AET, ZZAP) destroy Kell antigens but not Kx.

questions.teaching_point: The Kell glycoprotein and Xk protein are structurally distinct but covalently associated proteins on the RBC membrane; Kell antigen expression depends on the disulfide-stabilized conformation of the Kell glycoprotein's extracellular domain, so thiol-reducing agents (DTT, 2-ME, AET, ZZAP) abolish Kell reactivity while leaving Kx (on the separate Xk protein) intact — a useful serologic tool for distinguishing Kell-system antibodies from other high-prevalence antibodies.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old G2P1 woman at 24 weeks' gestation is found to have anti-K on her antibody identification panel. Titration reveals a comparatively low/modest titer. Paternal RBC typing shows the father is K-positive. Despite the low titer, the maternal-fetal medicine team elects to begin serial middle cerebral artery Doppler surveillance for fetal anemia rather than relying on the antibody titer alone to gauge severity, unlike the typical approach for anti-D alloimmunization. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiologic rationale for not relying on titer alone in anti-K hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN)?

options.text: Anti-K causes fetal anemia largely through destruction of erythroid precursor cells in the fetal marrow (in addition to peripheral hemolysis), because Kell glycoprotein is expressed on RBCs at a much earlier stage of erythropoiesis than Rh antigens, so severity correlates poorly with titer compared with anti-D disease
Anti-K is predominantly an IgM antibody that crosses the placenta poorly, so serum titers systematically underestimate the antibody concentration actually reaching the fetal circulation
The K antigen is not expressed on fetal RBCs until the third trimester, so early anemia in this pregnancy must reflect a cause unrelated to antibody-mediated RBC destruction
Anti-K fixes complement far more efficiently than anti-D, producing intravascular hemolysis that occurs too rapidly between blood draws to be reflected in periodic titers
Anti-K primarily destroys fetal platelet precursors rather than RBC precursors, so the anemia detected on ultrasound surveillance actually reflects a thrombocytopenia-related artifact

questions.explanation_correct: The source text explains that fetal anemia in anti-K HDFN results from suppression of erythropoiesis via destruction of erythroid precursor cells, in addition to destruction of circulating antigen-positive RBCs (the mechanism seen in anti-D HDFN). This occurs because Kell glycoprotein is expressed on fetal RBCs at a much earlier stage of erythropoiesis than Rh antigens. Consistent with this precursor-suppression mechanism, severe outcomes (including stillbirth) have been reported even at low anti-K titers, which is why paternal K typing and non-titer-based fetal surveillance are emphasized once anti-K is identified and the father is K-positive.

option_rationales.text: Correct. This is the mechanism explicitly described in the source: precursor-cell destruction from earlier Kell expression in erythropoiesis explains poor titer correlation with severity, distinguishing anti-K from anti-D HDFN.

Incorrect. The source indicates anti-K is an immune antibody induced by pregnancy or transfusion (an IgG-type immune response), not a poorly placenta-crossing IgM; the poor titer correlation is explained by precursor destruction, not by antibody class or transplacental passage failure.
Incorrect. The source states the K antigen can be detected on fetal RBCs as early as 10 weeks and is well developed at birth, directly contradicting a claim of no third-trimester expression.

Incorrect. The source does not describe anti-K as fixing complement more efficiently than anti-D or causing especially rapid intravascular hemolysis; the described mechanism is erythroid precursor suppression, not accelerated complement-mediated lysis.

Incorrect. The source specifically states Kell blood group antigens are found only on RBCs and have not been found on platelets, lymphocytes, granulocytes, or monocytes, so a platelet-precursor mechanism is not supported.

source_references.supports: Fetal anemia in anti-K HDFN arises from suppression of erythropoiesis (destruction of erythroid precursors) in addition to peripheral RBC destruction seen in anti-D HDFN; Kell glycoprotein is expressed earlier in erythropoiesis than Rh antigens; stillbirth has occurred at low anti-K titers; paternal K typing is recommended when anti-K is identified.
Kell antigens are found only on RBCs, not on platelets, lymphocytes, granulocytes, or monocytes; the K antigen is detectable on fetal RBCs as early as 10 weeks.

questions.teaching_point: Unlike anti-D HDFN, where severity correlates reasonably well with maternal titer via peripheral hemolysis, anti-K HDFN causes fetal anemia predominantly through suppression of erythropoiesis (destruction of erythroid precursors), because Kell glycoprotein appears earlier in erythroid development than Rh antigens. This means severe fetal anemia can occur even with low anti-K titers, so paternal K typing and non-titer-based surveillance (e.g., MCA Doppler) are used once anti-K is identified in pregnancy and paternal K-positivity is confirmed.

questions.stem: A blood bank physician is reviewing why individuals of West African descent, who are overwhelmingly of the Fy(a–b–) phenotype, are protected from certain forms of malaria. Their RBCs type as Fy(a–b–) and are also Fy:–3,–5, indicating a lack of Duffy glycoprotein on the red cell surface. Molecular studies have shown this phenotype arises from a mutation in the GATA promoter motif of the FY (ACKR1) gene that abolishes erythroid expression of the protein, while expression in other tissues is preserved. Which of the following best explains, at the level of Duffy protein structure and function, why these RBCs resist malarial invasion?

options.text: The Duffy glycoprotein is expressed exclusively on granulocytes and monocytes, the cell types actually invaded by Plasmodium vivax merozoites
Fya and Fyb antigens reside on a single extracellular loop that is destroyed by neuraminidase treatment, which normally allows merozoite attachment
The Duffy glycoprotein functions as the erythrocyte receptor for chemokines and is the same RBC receptor exploited by Plasmodium vivax merozoites for invasion
The GATA motif responsible for the Fy(a–b–) phenotype lies within the protein's third extracellular loop and directly binds the merozoite ligand
Disulfide bonds linking the first and second extracellular loops of the Duffy protein form the direct binding pocket for the Plasmodium vivax merozoite ligand

questions.explanation_correct: The Duffy glycoprotein (product of the FY/ACKR1 gene) is described in the sources as the erythrocyte chemokine receptor (DARC, Duffy antigen/receptor for chemokines) and as the receptor for the malarial parasite Plasmodium vivax. Because Fy(a–b–) RBCs lack this glycoprotein on the red cell surface (due to the GATA promoter mutation abolishing erythroid, but not other-tissue, expression), they resist invasion by P. vivax (and, as the passage notes in the context preceding P. vivax, by P. knowlesi as well) — the basis for the predominance of the Fy(a–b–) phenotype in persons of West African origin.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect — the source states Duffy antigens have NOT been found on platelets, lymphocytes, monocytes, or granulocytes; they are found on RBCs and on tissues such as brain, colon, endothelium, lung, spleen, thyroid, thymus, and kidney, not exclusively on granulocytes/monocytes.

Incorrect — the source explicitly states neuraminidase may reduce the molecular weight of Fya/Fyb but does NOT destroy antigenic activity, so this proposed mechanism contradicts the stated enzyme sensitivity profile.

Correct — supported by the reference list entries describing the Duffy antigen as 'the erythrocyte chemokine receptor' and as 'a receptor for the malarial parasite Plasmodium vivax,' consistent with the text noting Fy(a–b–) RBCs resist P. vivax infection.

Incorrect — the GATA motif is located in the gene's promoter region and disrupts mRNA transcription binding; it is a gene-regulatory element, not a domain of the translated extracellular protein, and the source does not describe it as ligand-binding.

Incorrect — the proposed Duffy protein structure does show disulfide bonds linking the NH2-terminal domain to the third loop and the first loop to the second loop, but the source does not state that this disulfide-bonded region is the direct merozoite-binding site; this extrapolates beyond the described structure.

source_references.source_id: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, 20th? ed.
source_references.supports: Fy(a–b–) RBCs resist infection by Plasmodium vivax (following mention of P. knowlesi); antigens not found on platelets/lymphocytes/monocytes/granulocytes but present on other tissues; neuraminidase reduces MW without destroying antigenic activity.

source_references.source_id: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices
source_references.supports: Proposed Duffy protein structure (Figure 8-9) with disulfide bonds linking the NH2-terminal domain to the third loop and the first to second loop; GATA promoter mutation abolishes erythroid Fyb expression while tissue expression is preserved.

Citation titles identifying the Duffy antigen as 'the erythrocyte chemokine receptor' and 'a receptor for the malarial parasite Plasmodium vivax,' and as 'Duffy antigen/receptor for chemokines (DARC)'.

questions.teaching_point: The Duffy glycoprotein is both an erythrocyte chemokine receptor (DARC) and the RBC receptor used by Plasmodium vivax (and P. knowlesi) for invasion; loss of erythroid expression via a GATA promoter mutation (not a structural protein change) explains the malaria-resistant Fy(a–b–) phenotype common in persons of West African descent, while structural features (extracellular loops, disulfide bonds, enzyme sensitivity) define serologic behavior of Fya/Fyb rather than the malaria-receptor function itself.

questions.stem: During pretransfusion antibody identification testing, a patient's plasma shows antiglobulin (IAT)-phase reactivity with multiple panel red blood cells, and more than one antibody specificity is suspected because the reaction pattern cannot be resolved on the standard panel alone. To help separate the underlying specificities, the technologist treats an aliquot of the panel cells with ficin and repeats testing. Reactivity attributable to one of the antibodies is completely lost against the ficin-treated cells, while reactivity from the other antibody or antibodies persists. Which of the following statements best characterizes the antibody whose reactivity was eliminated by ficin treatment?

options.text: It occurs about three times more frequently than anti-K and is usually IgM, reacting best at room temperature.
It is unaffected by ficin treatment of red cells but is destroyed by treatment with DTT alone.
It is usually IgG, reacts best by the antiglobulin (indirect antiglobulin) technique, and its reactivity is enhanced when testing is performed in a low-ionic-strength medium.

It is produced only by Fy(a–b–) individuals who lack Duffy glycoprotein on red cells and other tissues, and it reacts with essentially all red cells tested except Fy(a–b–) cells, regardless of enzyme treatment.
It is directed against an antigen that is also expressed on platelets, lymphocytes, and granulocytes, in addition to red blood cells.

questions.explanation_correct: Anti-Fya and anti-Fyb are the antibodies in the Duffy system most often encountered in routine pretransfusion testing. Because the Fya and Fyb antigens are destroyed by proteolytic enzymes such as ficin (but not by DTT alone), an antibody that disappears against ficin-treated panel cells while other reactivity persists is characteristic of anti-Fya (or anti-Fyb) — a technique the source text specifically describes as helpful for resolving multiple antibodies. These antibodies are usually IgG, react best at the antiglobulin phase, and show enhanced reactivity in a low-ionic-strength medium, matching option C.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect — the source states anti-Fya occurs three times LESS frequently than anti-K (not more), and anti-Fya/anti-Fyb are usually IgG reacting at the antiglobulin phase, not IgM reacting at room temperature.
Incorrect — this reverses the actual enzyme/DTT sensitivity profile: Fya and Fyb antigens ARE destroyed by ficin but are specifically NOT affected by DTT alone.

Correct — anti-Fya and anti-Fyb are usually IgG, react best at the antiglobulin phase, and their reactivity is enhanced in a low-ionic-strength medium, consistent with loss of reactivity on ficin-treated cells in this scenario.

Incorrect for this scenario — this describes anti-Fy3, which the source explicitly states is, unlike Fya and Fyb, NOT destroyed by enzymes; an antibody matching this description would NOT lose reactivity after ficin treatment.

Incorrect — the source states Duffy (Fya/Fyb) antigens have not been found on platelets, lymphocytes, monocytes, or granulocytes, though they have been identified in certain other body tissues (e.g., brain, colon, endothelium).

source_references.supports: Anti-Fya and anti-Fyb are usually IgG, react best at the antiglobulin phase, are enhanced in low-ionic-strength medium, are destroyed by ficin/papain/etc. but not by DTT alone, are not found on platelets/lymphocytes/monocytes/granulocytes, and anti-Fya occurs three times less frequently than anti-K; enzyme non-reactivity is noted as a technique to help identify other antibodies when multiple specificities are present.

Anti-Fy3 is made by Fy(a–b–) individuals who lack Duffy glycoprotein on RBCs and other tissues, reacts with all RBCs tested except Fy(a–b–) cells, and unlike Fya/Fyb, the Fy3 antigen is not destroyed by enzymes.

questions.teaching_point: Fya and Fyb antigens are destroyed by proteolytic enzymes (ficin, papain, bromelin, chymotrypsin, ZZAP) but not by DTT alone; loss of antiglobulin-phase reactivity against enzyme-treated panel cells is a practical technique to help resolve anti-Fya/anti-Fyb from other antibodies (e.g., Kidd) in a mixture, since anti-Fya/anti-Fyb are usually IgG, react best at the AHG phase, and are enhanced in LISS. This contrasts with anti-Fy3, which is enzyme-resistant.

questions.stem: A hematology reference laboratory identifies two siblings whose red cells type as Jk(a–b–). Pedigree analysis shows their parents type as Jk(a+b–) and Jk(a–b+), consistent with each parent carrying one copy of the rare silent Jk allele. Routine chemistries, including BUN, creatinine, and serum electrolytes, are normal in both siblings. When their renal concentrating capacity is specifically tested, which of the following findings is most likely to be identified, reflecting the physiologic role of the Kidd (JK) glycoprotein?

options.text: Elevated serum creatinine and BUN consistent with chronic kidney disease
A marked defect in the ability to maximally concentrate urine despite otherwise normal renal function tests
Persistent hyperkalemia reflecting abnormal renal electrolyte handling
Massive polyuria and hyponatremia diagnostic of complete nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
Recurrent gross hematuria from a tubular basement membrane defect

questions.explanation_correct: Family studies of the rare Jk(a–b–) null phenotype show that affected individuals have normal blood urea nitrogen, creatinine, and serum electrolytes, but a marked defect in their ability to concentrate urine has been demonstrated on specific renal physiology testing. This dissociation — normal standard renal chemistries but an isolated urinary concentrating defect — illustrates a physiologic function of the Kidd glycoprotein beyond its role as a red cell antigen.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. The source text specifically states that Jk(a–b–) individuals have normal blood urea nitrogen and creatinine, arguing against a chronic kidney disease picture.
Correct. The passage states that although BUN, creatinine, and serum electrolytes are normal, studies on Jk(a–b–) individuals showed a marked defect in their ability to concentrate urine.
Incorrect. Serum electrolytes are described as normal in this phenotype; no hyperkalemia or electrolyte abnormality is reported.

Incorrect and overstated. The source describes only a 'marked defect' in urinary concentrating ability, not the massive polyuria and hyponatremia of a complete diabetes insipidus syndrome; the passage does not characterize the defect as complete nephrogenic DI.
Incorrect. Hematuria and tubular basement membrane pathology are not mentioned anywhere in the source description of the Jk(a–b–) phenotype.

source_references.supports: States that Jk(a-b-) individuals have normal BUN, creatinine, and serum electrolytes but show a marked defect in ability to concentrate urine on specific testing.

questions.stem: During pretransfusion antibody identification, a patient's serum shows weak but reproducible reactivity at the antiglobulin phase with several reagent panel cells. Review of the panel sheet shows that every reactive cell is Jk(a+b–) and every nonreactive cell is either Jk(a+b+) or Jk(a–b+). The technologist suspects anti-Jka but wants to select a panel cell phenotype that will reliably exclude (rule out) this antibody rather than risk a false exclusion due to the antibody's known dosage effect. Which panel cell phenotype should be used to confidently rule out anti-Jka?

options.text: Jk(a+b+) cells, because heterozygous expression is the most commonly available phenotype and is considered sufficient for exclusion
Jk(a+b–) cells, because they carry a single (homozygous) dose of Jka and reliably detect weakly reactive, dosage-sensitive anti-Jka
Jk(a–b+) cells, because any reactivity with Jka-negative cells confirms the antibody is not anti-Jka
Jk(a–b–) cells, because the null phenotype eliminates any confounding effect of antigen dose
Ficin-treated Jk(a+b+) cells, because enzyme treatment converts single-dose antigen expression to double-dose expression

questions.explanation_correct: Kidd antibodies characteristically demonstrate dosage: they often react more strongly (or only) with RBCs carrying a double/homozygous dose of the corresponding antigen and may fail to react with heterozygous Jk(a+b+) cells. The source text explicitly instructs that anti-Jka should be ruled out only using Jk(a+b–) panel cells (and anti-Jkb only using Jk(a–b+) cells), because an anti-Jka that reacts only with Jk(a+b–) RBCs can give inconclusive results and falsely appear compatible/excluded when tested only against Jk(a+b+) cells.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect — this is precisely the pitfall described: Jk(a+b+) cells carry only a single dose of Jka, and a dosage-sensitive anti-Jka may be nonreactive with them even though the antibody is present, leading to a false rule-out.
Correct — the source states that anti-Jka should be ruled out only with Jk(a+b–) panel cells because these carry the antigen at a dose sufficient to reliably reveal a weak, dosage-sensitive antibody.
Incorrect — Jk(a–b+) cells lack Jka entirely; they are the appropriate cells for ruling out anti-Jkb, not anti-Jka, and would never react with anti-Jka regardless of whether the antibody is present.

Incorrect — Jk(a–b–) cells lack Jka (and Jkb) entirely, are exceedingly rare, and are not the panel cells described in the source for ruling out anti-Jka; they would not react with anti-Jka regardless of its presence.

Incorrect — enzyme treatment (ficin/papain) enhances reactivity of Kidd antibodies with antigen-positive cells but does not alter the underlying zygosity of the cell; Jk(a+b+) cells remain a single (heterozygous) dose of Jka even after enzyme treatment.

source_references.supports: Describes Kidd antibody dosage effect: stronger/exclusive reactivity with double-dose RBCs, potential nonreactivity with Jk(a+b+) cells, and the explicit instruction to rule out anti-Jka only with Jk(a+b–) cells (and anti-Jkb only with Jk(a–b+) cells), with Jk(a+b+) cells used as a positive control to confirm antisera can detect weak antigen expression.

questions.teaching_point: Kidd antibodies (anti-Jka, anti-Jkb) show dosage and are frequently weak and combined with other antibodies, making them notoriously difficult to detect. To avoid falsely ruling out a Kidd antibody, exclusion must be performed using antigen-positive, homozygous (single-antigen, double-dose) panel cells — Jk(a+b–) for anti-Jka and Jk(a–b+) for anti-Jkb — rather than heterozygous Jk(a+b+) cells, and crossmatch-compatible units should still be antigen-typed with commercial antisera (using Jk(a+b+) cells as a positive control) before release.
