questions.stem: A 58-year-old man is diagnosed with erythrodermic cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (Sézary syndrome), with diffuse erythema covering most of his body surface area and circulating malignant T cells with cerebriform nuclei on peripheral smear. His treating physician requests extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) as part of his management. Which of the following correctly pairs the ASFA indication category assigned to ECP for this specific presentation with the underlying mechanism of the procedure?

options.text: Category III; the buffy-coat layer is collected by leukapheresis, treated with 8-methoxypsoralen and ultraviolet A light to crosslink leukocyte DNA, and reinfused, inducing apoptosis of the malignant clone.

Category I; the buffy-coat layer is collected by leukapheresis, treated with 8-methoxypsoralen and ultraviolet A light to crosslink leukocyte DNA, and reinfused, inducing apoptosis of the malignant clone.
Category IV, since ECP has been reported as either of no benefit or harmful in cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and should be used only under an approved research protocol.
Category II, since ECP is designated a second-line treatment, alone or in conjunction with other therapies, for this disease.
Category I; the buffy coat is removed by centrifugation and discarded without any psoralen treatment, ultraviolet exposure, or reinfusion, permanently depleting the malignant T-cell clone.

questions.explanation_correct: Per the ASFA indications table referenced in the AABB Technical Manual, cutaneous T-cell lymphoma/Sézary syndrome is subdivided by clinical subtype: the erythrodermic form is Category I (apheresis as first-line treatment), while the nonerythrodermic form is Category III. The mechanism of ECP, described in both source texts, is collection of the buffy-coat layer by leukapheresis, treatment with 8-methoxypsoralen (8-MOP) and ultraviolet A (UVA) light, and reinfusion into the patient; this causes crosslinking of leukocyte DNA that prevents replication and induces apoptosis of the malignant T-cell clone. Option B correctly pairs the Category I designation for this erythrodermic presentation with this accurately described mechanism.

option_rationales.text: The mechanism described is accurate, but Category III is the designation given in the source table for nonerythrodermic CTCL, not the erythrodermic/Sézary syndrome presentation described, which is Category I.

Correct: erythrodermic CTCL/Sézary syndrome is Category I in the referenced ASFA table, and the mechanism (leukapheresis collection of buffy coat, 8-MOP plus UVA exposure, DNA crosslinking, apoptosis, reinfusion) matches both source passages exactly.

Category IV signifies apheresis is of no benefit or harmful and restricted to research protocols; the sources instead state photopheresis 'has been shown to be efficacious and has been approved by the FDA for the treatment of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma,' contradicting this option.

Category II is defined in the source as a second-line treatment designation, but this is not the category assigned to CTCL/Sézary syndrome in the referenced ASFA indications table; erythrodermic disease is Category I.

This option retains the correct Category I designation for erythrodermic disease but describes an incorrect mechanism: the sources explicitly state the collected cells are treated with 8-MOP and UVA light and then reinfused, not discarded after simple centrifugation without photoactivation.

source_references.locator: AABB Technical Manual, 20th ed., p.1 (retrieved passage)
source_references.supports: Table 25-8 lists cutaneous T-cell lymphoma/Sézary syndrome with erythrodermic subtype as Category I and nonerythrodermic subtype as Category III; also describes ECP mechanism (buffy-coat collection, 8-MOP, UVA, DNA crosslinking, apoptosis).

source_references.locator: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.411
source_references.supports: States photopheresis is FDA-approved and efficacious for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, and describes the mechanism: leukapheresis collection of buffy coat, treatment with 8-MOP and UVA light, crosslinking of leukocyte DNA leading to apoptosis, then reinfusion.

source_references.locator: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.405
source_references.supports: Defines the four ASFA indication categories (I first-line; II second-line; III individualized/not established; IV no benefit or harmful, research protocol only), used to construct the distractor category definitions.

questions.teaching_point: ECP's ASFA category for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma depends on clinical subtype: erythrodermic disease (including Sézary syndrome) is Category I, while nonerythrodermic disease is Category III. The mechanism underlying ECP for all indications is collection of the buffy coat, ex vivo treatment with 8-MOP and UVA light causing leukocyte DNA crosslinking, and reinfusion, inducing apoptosis of the treated cells.

group.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
group.topic_path: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events › Febrile, Allergic, and Non-Immune Transfusion Reactions › Anaphylactic and Anaphylactoid

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman is receiving an apheresis platelet transfusion. Ten minutes into the infusion, she develops diffuse urticaria that rapidly progresses to wheezing, profound hypotension, and shock. She remains afebrile and has no rigors throughout the episode. The transfusion is stopped immediately, and she is treated with epinephrine and supportive care with full recovery. Gram stain and culture of the residual platelet unit are subsequently negative. According to the severity-based classification of allergic transfusion reactions, which term best describes this patient's reaction?

options.text: Allergic reaction
Anaphylactoid reaction
Anaphylactic reaction
Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction
Transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection

questions.explanation_correct: Allergic transfusion reactions (ATRs) exist on a severity spectrum. The most severe category is characterized by profound hypotension and shock and is life-threatening; severe reactions of this type resulting in cardiopulmonary resuscitation or death are rare, with estimated frequencies of 1 per 20,000 to 1 per 47,000 units transfused. This patient's rapid progression from urticaria to wheezing, profound hypotension, and shock, without fever or a positive culture, fits this severe category rather than the milder allergic or moderately severe anaphylactoid categories, or the non-allergic reactions (FNHTR, TTBI).

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. Allergic reactions are generally mild to moderate and are limited to skin and gastrointestinal signs and symptoms (e.g., urticaria, pruritus, localized/generalized rash) — they do not include shock or profound hypotension as seen here.

Incorrect. Anaphylactoid reactions are moderately severe and include oral/throat symptoms, more severe GI symptoms, and respiratory complaints, but they do not encompass the profound hypotension and shock described in this severe presentation.

Correct. This severe, life-threatening presentation with profound hypotension and shock matches the most severe category of ATR, consistent with the source's description of rare, severe reactions that can require resuscitation.
Incorrect. FNHTRs present with fever and chills (managed with antipyretics) and are not associated with urticaria, wheezing, or shock; this patient was afebrile throughout.

Incorrect. Transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection typically presents with high fever (>38.5°C), rigors, hypotension, and tachycardia due to infusion of a contaminated product; this patient was afebrile with a negative culture, arguing against this diagnosis.

source_references.supports: Defines allergic, anaphylactoid, and anaphylactic reactions along a severity spectrum: allergic (mild-moderate, skin/GI), anaphylactoid (moderately severe, oral/throat/GI/respiratory), and the most severe category with profound hypotension and shock; notes severe reactions requiring resuscitation are rare (1/20,000-1/47,000 units).
Describes TTBI clinical presentation (high fever >38.5°C, rigors, hypotension, tachycardia) used to construct the negative distractor for transfusion-transmitted bacterial infection.

questions.teaching_point: Allergic transfusion reactions span a severity spectrum from mild allergic (skin/GI only) through anaphylactoid (moderate, with oral/throat, GI, and respiratory symptoms) to the most severe, life-threatening category marked by profound hypotension and shock; this must be distinguished clinically from FNHTR (fever/chills only) and TTBI (high fever, rigors, positive culture).

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with hereditary spherocytosis and chronic compensated hemolysis receives several units from a lot of solvent/detergent (SD)-treated pooled plasma for management of an acquired coagulopathy. Two weeks later she develops worsening anemia with reticulocytopenia, and testing reveals newly acquired parvovirus B19 viremia. The manufacturing record confirms the implicated plasma pool underwent standard SD treatment, a process that reliably inactivates HIV, HCV, and HBV in the same pool. Which of the following best explains why SD treatment failed to eliminate parvovirus B19 from this plasma-derivative pool?

options.text: Parvovirus B19 is a lipid-enveloped RNA virus whose envelope glycoproteins are unusually resistant to detergent disruption, unlike the envelope proteins of HIV, HCV, and HBV
Parvovirus B19 is a small, nonenveloped DNA virus, and SD treatment acts specifically by disrupting lipid envelopes, so nonenveloped viruses are intrinsically resistant to this process
SD treatment inactivates parvovirus B19 effectively, but the virus was reintroduced into the pool through contamination occurring downstream of processing
Parvovirus B19 rapidly mutates its capsid proteins during plasma pooling, generating SD-resistant variants that were not present in the original donor units
Nanofiltration performed as part of pathogen reduction selectively concentrates parvovirus B19 particles in the pool rather than removing them

questions.explanation_correct: The reference passages state that SD treatment inactivates lipid-enveloped agents such as HIV, HCV, and HBV, but that not all infectious agents are removed or inactivated by these processes. Parvovirus B19 is specifically described as a small, nonenveloped DNA virus that is extremely resistant to physical inactivation and can therefore persist in plasma-derivative products even after full SD processing. A separate passage reinforces that organic solvent/detergent methods inactivate lipid-enveloped viruses but are not effective against non-lipid-enveloped viruses such as parvovirus. This structural feature (absence of a lipid envelope) — not titer, mutation, or downstream contamination — is the reason SD treatment cannot reliably clear parvovirus B19.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect and internally contradictory: the source passages classify parvovirus B19 as a small, nonenveloped DNA virus, not a lipid-enveloped RNA virus. If it were lipid-enveloped, SD treatment would be expected to inactivate it, just as it does HIV, HCV, and HBV.

Correct. The passages explicitly identify parvovirus B19 as nonenveloped and note that SD treatment inactivates lipid-enveloped agents; a virus lacking a lipid envelope has no target for solvent/detergent disruption, explaining its persistence in plasma-derivative pools despite processing.

Not supported by the source material, which attributes B19 persistence to intrinsic resistance to physical inactivation rather than to a breach in downstream sterility or post-processing contamination.

Not supported; the passages give no indication that parvovirus B19 develops SD-resistant capsid mutations during pooling. Its resistance is described as an intrinsic property of the virus, not an acquired one.

Not supported; the passages mention nanofiltration only as one of several methods that can reduce pathogen infectivity in some manufacturing processes, with no description of it concentrating parvovirus B19 particles.

source_references.supports: States that SD treatment inactivates lipid-enveloped agents (HIV, HCV, HBV) but not all infectious agents are removed/inactivated by plasma-derivative manufacturing processes; describes parvovirus B19 as a small, nonenveloped DNA virus extremely resistant to physical inactivation that can persist in plasma-derivative products.

source_references.source_id: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices
source_references.supports: States that lipid-enveloped viruses (HIV, HBV, HCV, HTLV, EBV, CMV, HHV-6, HHV-8) are inactivated by organic solvents and detergents, but this process is not effective against non-lipid-enveloped viruses such as HAV and parvovirus.

questions.teaching_point: Solvent/detergent (SD) plasma treatment disrupts lipid envelopes and reliably inactivates enveloped viruses (HIV, HCV, HBV, HTLV, CMV, EBV), but it has no activity against nonenveloped viruses. Parvovirus B19, a small nonenveloped DNA virus, is the classic example of a pathogen that can persist in SD-treated and other plasma-derivative pools despite processing, which is why manufacturers additionally screen incoming plasma pools for high-titer B19 by NAT.

group.topic_path: 1. Clinical Practice › Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia › Drug-Induced Immune Hemolytic Anemia › Drug-Dependent Antibody Mechanism

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man receives a single intravenous prophylactic dose of ceftriaxone before a surgical procedure. Four days later he develops sudden flank pain and dark urine, and labs show an abrupt drop in hemoglobin with hemoglobinemia, hemoglobinuria, and new acute renal failure. The direct antiglobulin test is strongly positive for complement (C3) with little or no IgG detected. In the blood bank, the patient's serum does not react with untreated reagent red cells alone, but produces marked hemolysis/agglutination of the same untreated red cells when ceftriaxone is added directly to the test system. Which mechanism best explains these serologic and clinical findings?

options.text: Drug adsorption (penicillin-type/hapten) mechanism, in which antibody binds drug that is firmly attached to the red cell membrane and is detected only by testing serum against drug-treated red cells
Immune complex mechanism, in which antibody requires soluble drug present in vitro to react with untreated red cells, often fixes complement, and is associated with acute intravascular hemolysis
Autoantibody (autoimmune) mechanism, in which the drug induces a true warm autoantibody that reacts with untreated red cells and is reactive in an eluate even when the drug is absent
Nonimmunologic protein adsorption (NIPA), in which the drug causes nonspecific adsorption of plasma proteins onto the red cell membrane independent of true antibody production

questions.explanation_correct: The serologic pattern described (serum reactive with untreated red cells only when drug is added in vitro, complement-dominant DAT, and acute intravascular hemolysis with hemoglobinemia/hemoglobinuria and renal failure) matches the classic pattern for drug-dependent antibodies detected by testing untreated red cells in the presence of drug, as described for agents such as piperacillin and cephalosporins (anti-ceftriaxone is detected only by this method). The source text states that a drug or metabolite must be present in vitro for the antibody to react, that IgG and complement are detected (complement may be the only protein present), and that acute intravascular hemolysis with hemoglobinuria and common renal failure is the usual presentation — this reactivity is attributed to circulating drug or drug-plus-antibody complexes rather than a fixed hapten on the membrane, i.e., the immune complex mechanism.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. The drug adsorption (penicillin-type) mechanism is detected using drug-treated red cells (the drug is firmly bound to the membrane), and the DAT is usually IgG-positive with hemolysis developing gradually — not the untreated-cells-plus-drug reactivity and abrupt complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis seen here.

Correct. This matches the description of drug-dependent antibodies reactive with untreated red cells only in the presence of drug: IgG and/or complement (complement may be the only protein detected), a small/single dose can trigger reactivity, and acute intravascular hemolysis with hemoglobinemia, hemoglobinuria, and frequent renal failure is the usual presentation.

Incorrect. A true drug-induced warm autoantibody reacts with red cells independent of drug and would be expected to react in an eluate prepared without drug present in the system; here the serum is nonreactive against untreated cells without added drug, arguing against a true autoantibody.

Incorrect. NIPA is a nonimmunologic, antibody-independent process causing nonspecific protein adsorption onto red cells; it does not explain the drug-dependent serum reactivity and hemolytic/renal picture described, which reflects a true antibody-mediated reaction requiring drug in vitro.

source_references.supports: Describes drug-dependent antibodies reactive with untreated red cells only in the presence of drug: IgG and complement detected (complement may be only protein), drug must be present in vitro, acute intravascular hemolysis with hemoglobinemia/hemoglobinuria is usual, renal failure common, anti-ceftriaxone detected only by this method.
source_references.locator: p.1 (AABB Technical Manual, 20th ed., DAT/Immune Hemolysis section)

source_references.supports: Describes the contrasting drug adsorption (penicillin-type) pattern detected using drug-treated red cells, with DAT usually IgG-positive and gradual hemolysis, used to build distractor A.
source_references.locator: p.1 (AABB Technical Manual, 20th ed., DAT/Immune Hemolysis section)

source_references.supports: States that reactivity in some drug-dependent cases is attributed to circulating drug or drug-plus-antibody complexes rather than a true autoantibody, and that eluates are usually nonreactive when drug is absent, supporting the immune complex explanation and the autoantibody distractor rationale.
source_references.locator: p.1 (AABB Technical Manual, 20th ed., DAT/Immune Hemolysis section)

source_references.supports: Introduces the four classic serologic mechanisms of drug-induced positive DAT results: drug adsorption (penicillin-type), immune complex formation, autoantibody production, and nonimmunologic protein adsorption (NIPA), used as the option framework.
source_references.locator: p.1 (AABB Technical Manual, 20th ed., Theoretical Mechanisms of Drug-Induced Antibodies)

questions.teaching_point: Distinguishing the drug-dependent antibody mechanisms hinges on the in vitro testing pattern: drug adsorption (penicillin-type) antibodies react only with drug-treated red cells and cause gradual, usually extravascular hemolysis with an IgG-positive DAT, whereas immune-complex-type antibodies (e.g., piperacillin, cephalosporins including ceftriaxone) require soluble drug in the test system to react with untreated red cells, are complement-dominant on DAT, and classically cause abrupt, severe intravascular hemolysis with hemoglobinuria and renal failure, even after a single small dose.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old child with newly diagnosed acute lymphoblastic leukemia is midway through induction chemotherapy. On a routine complete blood count, the child is afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and has no overt signs or symptoms of anemia. The hematocrit is 22%. According to AABB pediatric RBC transfusion guidelines for patients older than 4 months, which of the following best describes the transfusion decision?

options.text: Not indicated — the pediatric threshold for chemotherapy-associated anemia is a hematocrit less than 18%, not less than 24%
Not indicated — under this guideline, RBC transfusion requires objective signs or symptoms of anemia in addition to the hematocrit criterion, which this patient lacks
Indicated — a hematocrit less than 24% in a patient currently receiving chemotherapy or radiotherapy is a listed transfusion indication, independent of symptoms
Indicated only if the patient also has sickle cell disease with a prior cerebrovascular accident, which is the sole hematocrit-independent indication for chemotherapy patients
Not indicated — transfusion during active chemotherapy is relatively contraindicated because of the risk of red cell alloimmunization

questions.explanation_correct: The AABB Technical Manual's pediatric transfusion guidelines (Table 24-4, for patients older than 4 months) list 'Hematocrit <24% and... while on chemotherapy/radiotherapy' as its own distinct indication for RBC transfusion, separate from the perioperative and chronic-anemia criteria that explicitly require signs/symptoms. This child's hematocrit of 22% while on chemotherapy therefore meets a listed indication for transfusion even without overt symptoms.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. The source lists a hematocrit threshold of <24% (not <18%) for the chemotherapy/radiotherapy indication.

Incorrect. Unlike the perioperative (4a) and chronic symptomatic anemia (4c) criteria, the 'while on chemotherapy/radiotherapy' criterion (4b) is listed under the hematocrit <24% threshold without an explicit requirement for signs or symptoms of anemia.
Correct. Table 24-4 explicitly lists hematocrit <24% while on chemotherapy/radiotherapy as an indication for RBC transfusion in patients older than 4 months.

Incorrect. Sickle cell disease with cerebrovascular accident is a separate, distinct listed indication (item 7a) in the same table; it does not gate transfusion eligibility for a leukemia patient on chemotherapy, who has an independent qualifying criterion (4b).

Incorrect. The source does not describe chemotherapy as a contraindication to transfusion; alloimmunization risk is discussed in the source specifically in the context of chronic transfusion in sickle cell disease, not as a reason to withhold transfusion during chemotherapy.

source_references.supports: Table 24-4, 'Transfusion Guidelines for RBCs in Patients Older than 4 Months,' lists as indication 4: 'Hematocrit <24% and: a. In perioperative period, with signs and symptoms of anemia. b. While on chemotherapy/radiotherapy. c. Chronic congenital or acquired symptomatic anemia,' distinguishing the chemotherapy/radiotherapy criterion from the symptom-requiring criteria.

questions.teaching_point: AABB pediatric RBC transfusion guidelines for patients >4 months list a hematocrit <24% while on chemotherapy or radiotherapy as its own qualifying indication for transfusion, distinct from criteria that require concurrent signs or symptoms of anemia.

group.topic_path: 1. Clinical Practice › Immune Thrombocytopenia › Drug-Induced Immune Thrombocytopenia › Drug-Induced Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura and Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome

questions.stem: A hospitalized patient on a medication known to cause thrombotic microangiopathy develops thrombocytopenia with schistocytes on the peripheral smear. Platelet transfusions are given, but the platelet count fails to rise as expected. While working up the poor platelet increment, the transfusion service physician reviews the standard classification of causes of platelet transfusion refractoriness and notes that drug-induced thrombotic microangiopathy (TTP; HUS) is listed separately from drug-dependent platelet antibodies. According to this classification, how is drug-induced TTP/HUS categorized as a cause of platelet refractoriness?

options.text: Immune cause, mediated by HLA antibodies
Immune cause, mediated by ABO incompatibility between donor platelets and the recipient
Nonimmune cause, resulting from platelet consumption within microvascular thrombi rather than antibody-mediated platelet destruction
Immune cause, mediated by drug-dependent autoantibodies directed against platelet antigens
Immune cause, mediated by human platelet antigen (HPA) antibodies

questions.explanation_correct: The AABB Technical Manual's table of causes of platelet transfusion refractoriness lists 'Thrombotic microangiopathy (TTP; HUS, drug-induced)' under the Nonimmune column, separate from the Immune column, which instead lists 'Drug-dependent autoantibodies' as its own distinct entry. This reflects that drug-induced TTP/HUS causes refractoriness through platelet consumption in microthrombi rather than through antibody-mediated platelet destruction, distinguishing it mechanistically from drug-induced immune thrombocytopenia.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. HLA antibodies are listed as an immune cause of refractoriness in the same table, but this reflects alloimmunization from prior transfusion or pregnancy, not drug-induced thrombotic microangiopathy.
Incorrect. ABO incompatibility is listed as an immune cause of refractoriness in the table but is unrelated to drug-induced TTP/HUS.

Correct. The table places 'Thrombotic microangiopathy (TTP; HUS, drug-induced)' under Nonimmune causes of refractoriness, reflecting consumption of platelets in microvascular thrombi rather than antibody-mediated destruction.

Incorrect. Drug-dependent autoantibodies are listed as a separate, distinct immune cause of refractoriness in the same table (representing drug-induced immune thrombocytopenia), not the same entity as drug-induced TTP/HUS.
Incorrect. HPA antibodies are listed as an immune cause of refractoriness in the table but are not the mechanism underlying drug-induced thrombotic microangiopathy.

source_references.supports: Table 15-3 (Causes of Platelet Refractoriness) lists 'Thrombotic microangiopathy (TTP; HUS, drug-induced)' under Nonimmune causes, separately from 'Drug-dependent autoantibodies,' which appears under Immune causes, along with HLA antibodies, ABO incompatibility, and HPA antibodies also listed as immune causes.

questions.teaching_point: Drug-induced TTP/HUS (thrombotic microangiopathy) is a nonimmune cause of platelet consumption and platelet transfusion refractoriness, mechanistically distinct from drug-dependent autoantibody-mediated (immune) drug-induced thrombocytopenia — an important distinction when working up refractoriness and when situating drug-induced TTP/HUS within the broader differential of drug-induced thrombocytopenic syndromes.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia is 3 weeks post allogeneic hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) transplant. He falls at home and develops active bleeding, prompting emergency transfusion of an ABO-compatible platelet unit that was not irradiated. Ten days later he develops high fever, a diffuse erythematous rash, profuse watery diarrhea, and pancytopenia that fails to respond to growth factor support or further transfusion. He ultimately dies. Which of the following, if performed on the platelet unit prior to transfusion, would most likely have prevented this fatal complication?

options.text: Leukoreduction of the platelet unit
Gamma or X-ray irradiation of the platelet unit
Selection of a CMV-seronegative platelet unit
Washing of the platelet unit prior to transfusion
Selection of an ABO-identical (rather than ABO-compatible) platelet unit

questions.explanation_correct: The clinical picture (fever, erythematous rash, watery diarrhea, and pancytopenia progressing to death) in a severely immunocompromised HPC transplant recipient is classic for transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD), caused by viable donor T lymphocytes in a cellular blood product engrafting and attacking host skin, gut, and marrow. The source text identifies transfusion-associated GVHD as one of the special transfusion challenges unique to HPC transplant recipients, and its accompanying review content specifically identifies irradiation of blood products (not leukoreduction, CMV-negative selection, or washing) as the intervention that prevents this outcome by inactivating donor T lymphocytes so they cannot proliferate in the recipient.

option_rationales.text: Leukoreduction lowers white cell counts to reduce febrile nonhemolytic reactions and CMV transmission risk, but the residual lymphocytes remain viable and capable of engraftment, so it does not prevent TA-GVHD.

Correct: irradiation inactivates donor T lymphocytes' ability to proliferate, which is the standard measure to prevent transfusion-associated GVHD in HPC transplant recipients, as reflected in the source material.
CMV-seronegative products address a separate special transfusion requirement in HPC recipients — reducing transfusion-transmitted CMV infection — not GVHD.
Washing removes plasma proteins and reduces allergic/anaphylactic transfusion reactions; it does not remove or inactivate the T lymphocytes responsible for TA-GVHD.

ABO selection (identical vs. compatible) addresses hemolytic transfusion reaction risk from ABO incompatibility, another listed special transfusion consideration in HPC recipients, but is unrelated to GVHD prevention.

source_references.locator: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.424
source_references.supports: States that HPC transplant recipients present unique transfusion-service challenges including transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease, and its embedded review item identifies irradiation of blood products as the intervention that prevents the fatal fever/rash/diarrhea/cytopenia presentation in an allogeneic HPC transplant recipient.

questions.teaching_point: In HPC (hematopoietic progenitor cell) transplant recipients, all cellular blood products must be irradiated to prevent transfusion-associated GVHD; leukoreduction, CMV-negative selection, and washing address different, non-overlapping transfusion risks.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man serves as a repeat directed apheresis donor, providing granulocytes over several consecutive days for a neutropenic relative with a bacterial infection unresponsive to antibiotics. During each collection, hydroxyethyl starch (HES) is added to the apheresis circuit as a red blood cell sedimenting agent to improve granulocyte yield. Which of the following statements about the use of HES in this donor is correct?

options.text: Some HES enters the donor's circulation during collection and can cause circulatory volume expansion, headache, and peripheral edema; because residual HES has been detected in donors up to a year after donation, collection facilities must limit the donor's cumulative HES dose over a defined interval.
Because HES is added only to the extracorporeal apheresis circuit, none of it enters the donor's bloodstream, so no additional donor monitoring or dose tracking is required.
HES functions as a mobilizing agent, stimulating the donor's bone marrow to release additional neutrophils, in the same way that corticosteroids or G-CSF do.
HES is fully cleared from the donor's circulation within 24 hours of donation, so cumulative dosing limits across repeat donations are unnecessary.
HES increases granulocyte yield by directly raising the donor's peripheral neutrophil count rather than by improving separation of blood layers during centrifugation.

questions.explanation_correct: HES is added directly to the apheresis circuit to improve separation of the buffy coat layer, reducing RBC contamination and improving granulocyte yield. Because it enters the extracorporeal circuit, some HES passes into the donor's circulation during collection. Its immediate colloid effects include circulatory volume expansion, headaches, and peripheral edema, and residual HES has been documented in donors up to a year after granulocyte apheresis. For this reason, collection facilities are required to control the maximum cumulative dose of HES (or any other sedimenting agent) given to a donor within a specified time interval, which is a key donor-safety consideration in repeat granulocyte apheresis donors.

option_rationales.text: Correct: matches the source directly — HES enters donor circulation, causes colloid-related side effects (volume expansion, headache, peripheral edema), persists up to a year, and requires cumulative dose control.

Incorrect: the source states that because HES is added directly to the apheresis circuit, some amount enters the donor's circulation during the collection procedure — it is not confined to an ex vivo circuit.

Incorrect: the source distinguishes HES (a sedimenting agent that improves layer separation) from corticosteroids or colony-stimulating factors, which are the agents used to stimulate the donor prior to the procedure and increase circulating neutrophil counts.

Incorrect: the source specifically notes residual HES has been reported in donors up to 1 year after granulocyte apheresis, which is exactly why cumulative dose limits over a specified interval are mandated.
Incorrect: the source attributes improved yield to HES enhancing separation of layers (with reduced RBC contamination) during centrifugation, not to a direct increase in the donor's neutrophil count.

source_references.locator: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.404
source_references.supports: Describes HES as a red blood cell sedimenting agent added to the apheresis circuit that improves layer separation/yield, enters donor circulation causing colloid side effects (volume expansion, headache, peripheral edema), persists up to 1 year post-donation, and requires facilities to control maximum cumulative donor dose over a specified interval.

questions.stem: A hospital transfusion service stores apheresis platelet units in a dedicated platelet incubator equipped with a continuous electronic temperature-monitoring device. During a routine equipment check, the electronic monitor is found to be malfunctioning and is removed from service pending repair. Staff begin recording the incubator's temperature by hand until the device is repaired or replaced. According to AABB Standards, what is the minimum acceptable frequency for these manual temperature recordings while the electronic monitoring device is out of service?

options.text: Continuously, with no gaps in recording permitted
At least every hour
At least every 4 hours
At least every 8 hours
At least once every 24 hours

questions.explanation_correct: AABB Standards require that electronic storage devices for blood and blood components be monitored continuously with temperature recorded at least every 4 hours, and that open storage areas have ambient temperature monitored and recorded at least every 4 hours. The AABB Technical Manual states that if an automated temperature-recording device is not used, temperatures of the blood storage environment must be recorded manually at least every 4 hours, and this requirement specifically includes ambient monitoring of platelets not stored in a platelet chamber or incubator. When the electronic monitor for the platelet incubator fails, manual recording every 4 hours satisfies this minimum standard.

option_rationales.text: Continuous, gap-free manual recording is not the standard requirement; the source specifies a defined maximum interval (4 hours), not continuous surveillance, for the manual/backup process.
Hourly recording is more frequent than required by AABB Standards and the Technical Manual, which specify a 4-hour interval as the minimum acceptable frequency.

Correct: both the AABB Standards (electronic device monitoring and open storage area monitoring) and the Technical Manual specify that temperature must be recorded at least every 4 hours, including manual recording when an automated device is unavailable.
Every 8 hours exceeds the maximum interval permitted; the source specifies at least every 4 hours, so this interval is too infrequent to meet the standard.
Once every 24 hours is far too infrequent under the cited standard and would risk failing to detect a temperature excursion that could compromise platelet quality and safety.

source_references.supports: States that if an automated temperature-recording device is not used, blood storage environment temperatures must be recorded manually at least every 4 hours, explicitly including ambient monitoring of platelets not kept in a chamber/incubator.

AABB Standard 5.1.9.3.1: electronic storage devices for blood and blood components shall be monitored continuously and temperature recorded at least every 4 hours; Standard 5.1.9.3.3: open storage area ambient temperature shall be monitored and recorded at least every 4 hours.
source_references.locator: p.? (AABB Standards for Blood Banks & Transfusion Services, 35th ed.)

questions.teaching_point: When automated/electronic temperature monitoring of blood component storage equipment (e.g., a platelet incubator) is unavailable, AABB Standards require manual temperature recording at a minimum frequency of at least every 4 hours to ensure storage conditions remain within the acceptable range.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is admitted to the burn intensive care unit after sustaining third-degree burns over 40% of his total body surface area in a house fire. The burn surgery team requests allograft skin from the hospital-affiliated tissue bank to provide temporary coverage of the excised wound bed while donor sites are prepared for autografting. The blood bank medical director is consulted about what compatibility testing, if any, must be completed before the skin allograft can be released to the operating room. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: No blood type or tissue (HLA) match between the donor and recipient is required before release of the skin allograft.
ABO/Rh typing of the donor skin must be confirmed to match the recipient's blood type before release, just as for red blood cell transfusion.
Full donor-recipient HLA matching is required before release, as is done for solid organ transplantation.
A serologic crossmatch between the donor tissue and the recipient's serum must be performed before release, analogous to red cell compatibility testing.

questions.explanation_correct: According to the source, tissue bank products such as skin grafts belong to a group of tissues that are useful 'regardless of the donor's or patient's HLA type,' and explicitly, 'No blood type or tissue match is required.' Skin allografts used for burn coverage (as well as other loss of large areas of skin from trauma, pressure sores, or nonhealing ulcers) are therefore released without ABO/Rh matching, HLA matching, or crossmatching, unlike cellular blood components such as red cells.

option_rationales.text: Correct. The source states plainly that for the majority of these tissue bank products, including skin, no blood type or tissue (HLA) match is required, distinguishing them from cellular blood components.
Incorrect. ABO/Rh matching is a red blood cell compatibility requirement; the source specifically states no blood type match is required for these tissue products.
Incorrect. The source states these tissues are useful 'regardless of the donor's or patient's HLA type,' so HLA matching is not required, unlike solid organ transplantation.
Incorrect. No crossmatch process is described for tissue bank products in the source; the 'no blood type or tissue match required' statement excludes this type of serologic testing.

source_references.supports: States that skin (used widely for burn victims and for surgical/traumatic loss of large areas of skin) is one of the tissue bank products for which 'No blood type or tissue match is required,' regardless of donor or patient HLA type.

questions.teaching_point: Tissue bank products such as skin allografts (and other non-vascularized tissues like sclera) are released without ABO/Rh typing, HLA matching, or crossmatching between donor and recipient, in contrast to cellular blood components; many such tissues come in standardized forms usable in any patient irrespective of blood type or HLA type.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man sustains 45% total body surface area burns and is undergoing initial fluid resuscitation in the burn unit. In addition to crystalloid, the team wants to add a synthetic colloid volume expander valued specifically for remaining in the intravascular space the longest between infusions. Based on the pharmacologic properties described for synthetic volume expanders, which of the following solutions has an IV half-life of more than 24 hours?

options.text: Dextran
Hydroxyethyl starch (HES)
Ringer's lactate
Normal (isotonic) saline
Albumin 5% solution

questions.explanation_correct: The source describes dextran and HES as the two synthetic colloid solutions used as volume expanders in hemorrhagic shock and burn patients. Dextran is prepared as a 6% or 10% solution with a half-life of 6 hours, while HES is available as a 6% solution with an IV half-life of more than 24 hours, making HES the colloid with the longest described intravascular persistence.

option_rationales.text: Dextran is a colloid used in burn/shock resuscitation, but the source gives it a half-life of only about 6 hours, far shorter than the >24-hour figure attributed to HES.
Correct. HES is explicitly described as a 6% solution with an IV half-life of more than 24 hours, the longest intravascular persistence of the agents discussed in the source.
Ringer's lactate is classified in the source as a crystalloid, not a colloid; crystalloids are described as having poor intravascular retention and being easily excreted, unlike colloids.

Normal isotonic saline is grouped with Ringer's lactate as a crystalloid in the source; crystalloids readily cross the capillary membrane and are easily excreted, so they do not provide prolonged intravascular volume support.

Albumin is described elsewhere in the source as a plasma-derived colloid available in 5% or 25% solutions, but no IV half-life figure is given for it; the >24-hour half-life in the source is attributed specifically to HES.

source_references.supports: States dextran (6% or 10% solution) has a half-life of 6 hours and HES (6% solution) has an IV half-life of more than 24 hours; both are colloids used as volume expanders in hemorrhagic shock and burn patients, contrasted with crystalloids (Ringer's lactate, normal saline).

questions.teaching_point: Among synthetic colloids used for burn/hemorrhagic shock resuscitation, HES provides substantially longer intravascular persistence (half-life >24 hours) than dextran (~6 hours), while crystalloids (Ringer's lactate, normal saline) are rapidly redistributed and excreted, giving only transient volume expansion.

group.topic_path: 10. Surgery Patients › Transfusion Therapy for Trauma and Burn Patients › Shock › Patients with Thermal Injuries (Burns) › Initial Resuscitations › Fluid Therapy › Crystalloid

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man sustains a 40% total body surface area thermal burn. As part of initial fluid resuscitation, the treating team selects a crystalloid solution (Ringer's lactate) rather than a colloid solution such as albumin or hydroxyethyl starch. Which of the following properties is characteristic of the crystalloid solution chosen for this patient?

options.text: Superior intravascular volume retention compared with colloid solutions, producing prolonged plasma expansion
Poor intravascular retention, with capillary leakage that commonly produces peripheral edema
Frequent allergic and anaphylactic reactions that require premedication before infusion
Substantially higher cost than colloid solutions such as hydroxyethyl starch
Poor renal excretion, requiring dose adjustment in patients with renal impairment

questions.explanation_correct: Crystalloid solutions such as Ringer's lactate and normal saline rapidly cross the capillary membrane, which lets them expand plasma volume quickly but also means they are poorly retained intravascularly. Per the source's comparison of crystalloid vs colloid solutions, crystalloids have 'poor' intravascular retention and 'common' peripheral edema, in contrast to colloids, which have 'good' retention and only 'possible' peripheral edema. This capillary permeability/poor retention profile is precisely what makes crystalloids useful for rapid plasma volume expansion in burn patients, at the cost of more peripheral edema.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect — the source's comparison table lists colloid (not crystalloid) as having 'good' intravascular retention; crystalloid retention is characterized as 'poor.'
Correct — the source states crystalloids rapidly cross the capillary membrane (poor intravascular retention) and lists 'common' peripheral edema for crystalloids versus 'possible' for colloids.
Incorrect — the source lists allergic reactions as 'absent' for crystalloid solutions (versus 'rare' for colloids), the opposite of frequent allergic/anaphylactic reactions.
Incorrect — the source's table lists crystalloid cost as 'inexpensive' and colloid cost as 'expensive,' the reverse of this option.
Incorrect — the source lists crystalloids as 'easily excreted' ('yes'), not poorly excreted, distinguishing them from colloids ('no').

source_references.supports: Describes crystalloids (Ringer's lactate, normal saline) as rapidly crossing the capillary membrane to increase plasma volume, useful in burn patients; and Table 15-4 comparing crystalloid vs colloid on intravascular retention (poor vs good), peripheral edema (common vs possible), excretion (easily excreted vs not), allergic reactions (absent vs rare), and cost (inexpensive vs expensive).

questions.teaching_point: In burn resuscitation, crystalloids (e.g., Ringer's lactate) are chosen for their ability to rapidly cross the capillary membrane and expand plasma volume, but this same permeability underlies their poor intravascular retention and higher propensity for peripheral edema compared with colloids, which are better retained intravascularly but more expensive and carry a small risk of allergic reaction.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old menstruating woman presents for her fourth whole blood donation of the year at a community blood drive. She passes the predonation hemoglobin screen and donates without any acute complication. As part of the center's donor iron-monitoring program, a predonation ferritin sample obtained at this visit later returns with a low ferritin level; the donor remains asymptomatic. According to AABB Technical Manual guidance on mitigating iron deficiency in blood donors, which of the following is the most effective approach for managing this donor's iron status going forward?

options.text: Place the donor on extended deferral from future red cell donation without providing her any information about the ferritin result or her iron status
Take no further action, since iron deficiency without anemia does not affect donor well-being
Permanently defer the donor from all future donations of any blood component, including plasma and platelet apheresis
Notify the donor of the low ferritin result and offer her the option of iron supplementation or delaying her next red cell donation
Refer the donor directly for bone marrow aspirate and biopsy to determine the cause of the low ferritin level

questions.explanation_correct: The AABB Technical Manual describes ferritin testing coupled with donor notification as a successful strategy: donors found to have low ferritin levels should be notified and offered the option of iron supplementation or delaying further (red cell) donation. Iron supplementation (e.g., an over-the-counter multivitamin with about 19 mg elemental iron, or iron tablets with about 38 mg elemental iron) has been shown to improve iron stores and hemoglobin, with adverse effects indistinguishable from placebo.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. The source explicitly states that extended deferrals without providing information about ferritin or iron supplementation will not restore iron levels in a reasonable time.

Incorrect. The source notes that both physical issues and impaired cognitive function have been suggested in individuals with low iron stores even in the absence of anemia, so low ferritin is not simply disregarded.
Incorrect. The passage describes low ferritin as the basis for an extended deferral specifically from donations that include red cell components, not a permanent deferral from all donation types.
Correct. This mirrors the described successful approach: perform ferritin testing, notify donors with low levels, and offer iron supplementation or delay of further donation.

Incorrect. The source indicates donors with an abnormally low or high ferritin level should be referred to their health-care provider for evaluation as appropriate; it does not describe automatic referral for bone marrow biopsy.

source_references.supports: Describes ferritin testing and donor notification with option of iron supplementation or delayed donation as a successful strategy; notes extended deferral without information will not restore iron levels; notes low ferritin can justify extended deferral from red-cell-containing donations; notes physical/cognitive effects of low iron even without anemia; notes referral to health-care provider for abnormal ferritin.

questions.teaching_point: Postdonation iron deficiency mitigation in frequent blood donors relies on ferritin testing combined with donor notification and either iron supplementation or delayed red-cell donation; uninformed deferral alone is ineffective, and low ferritin without anemia still warrants attention rather than dismissal.

group.topic_path: 11. Biovigilance and Transfusion-Related Immunomodulation › Biovigilance/Hemovigilance › Scope › Transfusion Recipients

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He is hypotensive and tachycardic with ongoing hemorrhage, and immediate red blood cell transfusion is deemed medically necessary before his ABO/RhD type can be determined or a sample can be tested. Which of the following should the transfusion service issue to this recipient?

options.text: Uncrossmatched group O, RhD-positive RBCs
Uncrossmatched group O, RhD-negative RBCs
Group AB, RhD-positive RBCs
No RBCs until the ABO/RhD type is confirmed by testing
Type-specific, fully crossmatched RBCs based on a rapid antibody screen

questions.explanation_correct: When a recipient's ABO group is unknown and transfusion is emergently required, uncrossmatched group O RBCs are issued. Because this recipient is a male (not of childbearing potential), AABB/Choosing Wisely guidance favors reserving the limited group O, RhD-negative inventory for females of childbearing potential and known group O, RhD-negative patients; group O, RhD-positive RBCs should be used for emergency transfusion of males and females who are not of childbearing potential.

option_rationales.text: Correct: the source states group O, RhD-positive RBCs should be used for emergency transfusions to males and females who are not of childbearing potential, in order to conserve the limited group O, RhD-negative inventory.

Incorrect: RhD-negative RBCs are preferentially reserved for females of childbearing potential (or patients known to be group O, RhD-negative), per the described inventory-management strategy; this recipient is male.
Incorrect: group AB RBCs are not the emergency-release product of choice; when the ABO group is unknown, uncrossmatched group O RBCs (or low-titer group O Whole Blood) are issued.

Incorrect: waiting for a confirmed type contradicts the emergency-release principle; if transfusion is medically necessary, blood may be released before pretransfusion testing is complete, with appropriate documentation.

Incorrect: a full crossmatch requires time that is not available in this emergency scenario; uncrossmatched group O RBCs are issued first, and compatibility testing is begun and completed promptly thereafter.

source_references.supports: Emergency-release procedure: issue uncrossmatched group O RBCs (or low-titer group O Whole Blood) when the recipient's ABO group is unknown, with RhD-negative RBCs preferred for females of childbearing potential and RhD-positive RBCs acceptable for other recipients; begin and promptly complete compatibility testing.

AABB/Choosing Wisely emphasis on selective use of group O, RhD-negative RBCs for known group O, RhD-negative patients and for emergency transfusion of females of childbearing potential, with group O, RhD-positive RBCs used for emergency transfusion of males and non-childbearing-potential females.

group.topic_path: 15. Obstetric and Pediatric Patients › Congenital Disorders of Clotting and Anticoagulant Protein › Hemophilia A, B, and von Willebrand Disease › Congenital Disorders of Non-Hemophilia Clotting Proteins

questions.stem: A full-term neonate is diagnosed with a severe, autosomal recessive coagulation protein deficiency. The disorder is characterized by a distinctive pattern of hemorrhage in the neonatal period that, if left untreated, progresses to a lifelong bleeding diathesis. Which of the following congenital deficiencies best fits this description?

options.text: Antithrombin deficiency
Protein C deficiency
Protein S deficiency
Factor XIII deficiency
Congenital fibrinogen deficiency

questions.explanation_correct: Factor XIII deficiency is described in the source as a severe autosomal-recessive bleeding disorder associated with a characteristic pattern of neonatal hemorrhage and a lifelong bleeding diathesis. This distinguishes it from the other congenital non-hemophilia clotting/anticoagulant protein disorders listed, which are hypercoagulable (thrombotic) conditions rather than bleeding conditions. Treatment options include human plasma-derived factor XIII concentrate and recombinant factor XIII, the latter a homodimer of factor XIII A-subunits indicated for prevention of bleeding in congenital factor XIII A-subunit deficiency.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. The source states that hereditary antithrombin deficiency is associated with venous thromboses (a hypercoagulable, pro-thrombotic state), not a neonatal bleeding syndrome.
Incorrect. Protein C is a vitamin K-dependent protein whose deficiency (hereditary or acquired) leads to a hypercoagulable state with a tendency toward thrombosis, not hemorrhage.

Incorrect. Protein S functions as a cofactor for activated protein C in inactivating factors V and VIII; its deficiency likewise produces a hypercoagulable, thrombosis-prone state rather than a bleeding disorder.
Correct. The source specifically describes Factor XIII deficiency as a severe autosomal-recessive bleeding disorder with a characteristic neonatal hemorrhage pattern and lifelong bleeding diathesis.

Incorrect. The source mentions congenital fibrinogen deficiency only as a rare indication for fibrinogen replacement (e.g., via cryoprecipitate); it is not described with the characteristic autosomal-recessive neonatal hemorrhage pattern attributed to Factor XIII deficiency.

source_references.supports: Factor XIII deficiency is a severe autosomal-recessive bleeding disorder with a characteristic pattern of neonatal hemorrhage and lifelong bleeding diathesis; treated with human-derived or recombinant factor XIII concentrate.
Hereditary antithrombin deficiency is associated with venous thromboses; protein C and protein S deficiency (hereditary or acquired) lead to a hypercoagulable state with tendency toward thrombosis.
Congenital fibrinogen deficiency is mentioned as a rare condition requiring fibrinogen replacement (e.g., cryoprecipitate), without description of a characteristic neonatal hemorrhage pattern.

questions.teaching_point: Among congenital non-hemophilia clotting protein disorders, Factor XIII deficiency is distinctive as an autosomal-recessive bleeding (not thrombotic) disorder presenting with a characteristic neonatal hemorrhage pattern and lifelong bleeding diathesis, in contrast to antithrombin, protein C, and protein S deficiencies, which are hypercoagulable/thrombotic conditions.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with multiple myeloma undergoes hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) mobilization with G-CSF in preparation for autologous HPC apheresis collection. On the planned day of collection, her peripheral blood CD34+ cell count is checked to assess mobilization adequacy and is found to be low, predicting a poor apheresis yield. Which of the following interventions is most likely to increase the HPC(A) collection yield in this patient?

options.text: Add plerixafor to the ongoing G-CSF regimen
Switch the apheresis anticoagulant from ACD-A to heparin alone
Reduce the total blood volume (TBV) processed to less than one TBV
Discontinue mobilization and proceed directly to a marrow harvest instead
Reduce the G-CSF dose to limit apheresis-related side effects

questions.explanation_correct: Peripheral blood CD34+ cell count is used to monitor mobilization adequacy before and during apheresis. When mobilization is poor, the source material states that patients may benefit from higher-dose G-CSF and/or the addition of plerixafor to G-CSF, and that randomized trials in myeloma and lymphoma patients showed plerixafor added to G-CSF can double circulating CD34+ HPCs, increasing collection yield and reducing the number of apheresis procedures needed.

option_rationales.text: Correct: the source states plerixafor in combination with G-CSF significantly increases HPC collection yields compared to G-CSF alone and can double circulating CD34+ cells in poorly mobilizing patients.

Incorrect: choice of anticoagulant (ACD-A vs. heparin) affects the toxicity profile of the apheresis procedure (citrate toxicity/hypocalcemia vs. bleeding/HIT risk), not the number of CD34+ cells mobilized or collected.

Incorrect: standard HPC(A) collection already processes 2-3x TBV (about 10-12 L in an adult), and large-volume leukapheresis (3-6x TBV) is used specifically to collect higher CD34+ numbers; processing less than one TBV would reduce, not improve, yield.

Incorrect: the passage discusses salvage of poor peripheral mobilization with pharmacologic strategies (higher-dose G-CSF, plerixafor), not abandonment of peripheral collection in favor of marrow harvest.
Incorrect: the source indicates poor mobilizers may benefit from higher-dose G-CSF, not a reduced dose; lowering the dose would not be expected to improve a low CD34+ count.

source_references.supports: States that patients with poor mobilization may benefit from higher-dose G-CSF and/or addition of plerixafor, and that plerixafor combined with G-CSF significantly increases HPC collection yields and can double circulating CD34+ cell counts, reducing the number of apheresis procedures needed.

Describes standard apheresis processing 2-3x TBV (10-12 L) versus large-volume leukapheresis (3-6x TBV) used to increase CD34+ cell yield, supporting why reducing processed volume would not increase yield.

questions.teaching_point: Peripheral blood CD34+ cell count monitoring guides decisions about mobilization adequacy and timing of apheresis; poor mobilizers can be salvaged with higher-dose G-CSF and/or addition of plerixafor, which can double circulating CD34+ HPCs and improve collection yield, reducing the number of apheresis procedures required.

group.entity: Collection of Lymphocytes for Infusion (Donor Lymphocyte Infusion [DLI])
group.topic_path: 16. Hematopoietic Progenitor Cell (HPC) Transplantation › HPC Sources and Collection › HPC Apheresis – Characteristics and Adverse Effects › Mobilization Regimens › Collection of Lymphocytes for Infusion (Donor Lymphocyte Infusion [DLI])

questions.stem: A patient develops relapsed chronic myeloid leukemia several months after allogeneic hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) transplantation. The transplant team elects to collect a cellular product from the original HPC donor for adoptive immunotherapy intended to harness a graft-versus-leukemia effect, rather than to re-establish hematopoiesis. Which of the following best describes the product and collection method used for this donor lymphocyte infusion (DLI)?

options.text: Bone marrow harvested by needle aspiration from the donor's iliac crest
CD34+ selected stem cells collected by apheresis
Mononuclear cells collected by apheresis from the donor (MNC-A)
Umbilical cord blood mononuclear cells
Granulocytes collected by apheresis after G-CSF/dexamethasone mobilization

questions.explanation_correct: The source states that mononuclear cells (MNC-A) may be procured from apheresis donations for use in donor lymphocyte infusions (DLIs), which is a form of adoptive immunotherapy geared toward a graft-versus-leukemia effect. DLI therefore consists of donor mononuclear cells collected by apheresis, not a stem-cell-enriched or marrow-derived product.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. Bone marrow needle aspiration is the collection method for an HPC-marrow graft used for hematopoietic engraftment; it is not how DLI cells are procured, and marrow harvest requires anesthesia and carries higher donor risk than apheresis.

Incorrect. CD34+ selection enriches for hematopoietic stem cells to promote engraftment; DLI instead relies on the lymphocyte/mononuclear cell content of an apheresis collection to produce an immunologic graft-versus-leukemia effect.

Correct. The source explicitly describes MNC-A (mononuclear cells procured from apheresis donations) as the product used for DLI, a form of adoptive immunotherapy directed at a graft-versus-leukemia effect.

Incorrect. Umbilical cord blood is a distinct allogeneic HPC graft source with its own characteristic advantages and disadvantages (e.g., lower acute GVHD risk, longer time to engraftment); it is not the source of cells for a DLI, which comes from the original HPC donor.
Incorrect. Granulocyte apheresis collection yields a product used to treat severe neutropenia or infection, not an adoptive immunotherapy product intended to elicit a graft-versus-leukemia effect.

source_references.source_id: Harmening, Modern Blood Banking & Transfusion Practices, p.419
source_references.supports: States that mononuclear cells (MNC-A) may be procured from apheresis donations for use in donor lymphocyte infusions (DLIs), described as a form of adoptive immunotherapy geared toward graft-versus-leukemia effect.

questions.teaching_point: Donor lymphocyte infusion (DLI) is a mononuclear cell product collected by apheresis (MNC-A) from the original HPC donor, used as adoptive immunotherapy to induce a graft-versus-leukemia effect — distinct from HPC-marrow, CD34+-selected, cord blood, or granulocyte products.

group.topic_path: 16. Hematopoietic Progenitor Cell (HPC) Transplantation › HPC Sources and Collection › HPC-Marrow

questions.stem: A transplant physician is selecting an allogeneic HPC graft source for a patient with an HLA-matched sibling donor. Weighing the options, the team decides to prioritize the lowest possible risk of graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) in the recipient, and accepts that the donor will need to undergo a collection procedure requiring anesthesia and carrying a higher rate of donor adverse events than an outpatient apheresis collection. Which HPC graft source is being selected for this donor-recipient pair?

options.text: HPC-Apheresis
HPC-Marrow
HPC-Cord Blood
Donor lymphocyte infusion (apheresis-derived mononuclear cells, MNC-A)

questions.explanation_correct: According to Table 19-1 in Harmening, HPC-marrow carries a lower risk of GVHD in the recipient than HPC-apheresis, but its collection (bone marrow harvest) involves a higher risk of adverse events to the donor than HPC-apheresis and requires anesthesia. This matches both criteria in the vignette: minimizing recipient GVHD risk while accepting greater donor procedural risk, including anesthesia.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. HPC-apheresis has a higher risk of GVHD in the recipient than HPC-marrow (though it offers faster neutrophil/platelet engraftment and a better graft-versus-leukemia effect), and its outpatient collection does not require anesthesia, so it does not fit either criterion in the vignette.

Correct. Table 19-1 lists 'lower risk of GVHD than HPC-apheresis' as an advantage of HPC-marrow, while its listed disadvantage is that 'donation involves higher risk of adverse events than HPC-apheresis and requires anesthesia' -- precisely the trade-off described.

Incorrect. HPC-cord blood carries a lower risk of acute GVHD and poses no risk at all to the donor (it is collected postpartum), which is the opposite of a procedure requiring anesthesia and carrying donor adverse-event risk; it also has a longer time to engraftment.

Incorrect. MNC-A collected by apheresis is used for donor lymphocyte infusions, a form of adoptive immunotherapy aimed at a graft-versus-leukemia effect, not as the primary hematopoietic graft source for engraftment.

source_references.supports: Table 19-1 (Commonly Used Allogeneic HPC Products) lists HPC-marrow's advantage as lower GVHD risk than HPC-apheresis and its disadvantage as higher donor adverse-event risk requiring anesthesia; also lists HPC-apheresis and HPC-cord blood trade-offs and describes MNC-A as used for donor lymphocyte infusions.

group.entity: Cord Blood Banking Donor Eligibility, Collection Methods, Processing, Testing, and Cryopreservation
group.topic_path: 16. Hematopoietic Progenitor Cell (HPC) Transplantation › HPC Sources and Collection › HPC Cord Blood › Characteristics and Methods › Cord Blood Banking Donor Eligibility, Collection Methods, Processing, Testing, and Cryopreservation

questions.stem: A cellular therapy processing laboratory is asked to release two cryopreserved umbilical cord blood units for a planned double HPC(CB) transplant in a 52-year-old man with relapsed acute myelogenous leukemia. His body weight is too large for a single banked cord blood unit to provide an adequate cell dose, so the transplant center has elected to proceed with a double-unit infusion. According to criteria used by many transplant centers selecting units for double HPC(CB) transplantation, what minimum total nucleated cell (TNC) dose, measured at the time of cryopreservation, should each individual cord blood unit provide?

options.text: 1 × 10^5 CD34+ cells/kg per unit
1.5 × 10^7 total nucleated cells/kg per unit
2.5 × 10^7 total nucleated cells/kg per unit
3 × 10^7 total nucleated cells/kg per unit

questions.explanation_correct: For double HPC(CB) transplants, many centers require that each individual cord blood unit meet a minimum TNC count of 1.5 × 10^7 cells/kg (of recipient body weight) at the time of cryopreservation. This per-unit threshold is lower than the minimum TNC dose accepted for a single HPC(CB) transplant, since two units are combined to reach an adequate total cell dose for the recipient.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect cell type for this criterion: 1 × 10^5 CD34+ cells/kg per unit is the arbitrary precryopreservation CD34+ cell dose some centers use as a minimum for each unit in double HPC(CB) transplantation, not the TNC threshold, which is a distinct measurement obtained by different methodology.
Correct. Some centers require each HPC(CB) unit in a double-unit transplant to have a minimum TNC count of 1.5 × 10^7/kg at cryopreservation.

This is the cryopreserved TNC dose (2.5 × 10^7 cells/kg) typically accepted as the minimum cell dose for successful engraftment in single HPC(CB) transplants, not the lower per-unit threshold used when two units are combined for a double transplant.

According to several reports, a minimum TNC count of 3 × 10^7/kg at cryopreservation is cited as generally needed to achieve engraftment, but this figure is not the specific per-unit threshold applied when selecting units for double HPC(CB) transplantation.

source_references.supports: States that a minimum TNC count of 3 x 10^7/kg at cryopreservation is generally cited for engraftment; that for double HPC(CB) transplants some centers require each unit to have a minimum TNC of 1.5 x 10^7/kg at cryopreservation; that 2.5 x 10^7 TNC/kg is typically accepted as the minimum for single HPC(CB) transplants; and that an arbitrary CD34+ dose of 1 x 10^5/kg per unit is used by some centers for double transplants.

questions.teaching_point: Cord blood banks and transplant centers apply distinct, source-specific minimum cell-dose thresholds when selecting HPC(CB) units, measured at cryopreservation: a per-unit TNC threshold for double-unit transplants (1.5 x 10^7/kg) is lower than the single-unit TNC threshold (2.5 x 10^7/kg), and a separate general engraftment threshold (3 x 10^7/kg) and CD34+-based threshold (1 x 10^5/kg per unit) exist alongside these TNC criteria; these numeric thresholds are easily confused and are a classic board-testing point.

group.entity: Donor Selection for Transplant (Related and Unrelated)

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia in first remission has no HLA-identical sibling. An unrelated donor search identifies two candidates who are both 8/8 matched at high-resolution HLA-A, -B, -C, and -DRB1 typing and are matched to the patient for ABO group and CMV serostatus. Donor A is a healthy 24-year-old man; Donor B is a healthy 52-year-old man. Both are medically eligible to donate. All else being equal, which factor is most likely to be associated with improved recipient survival in this scenario?

options.text: Selecting Donor B, because older unrelated donors have shown superior graft function once HLA and ABO compatibility are established

Considering the two donors equivalent, because killer cell immunoglobulin-like receptor (KIR) status is the only donor variable shown to independently affect survival once ABO and CMV matching are achieved
Selecting Donor A, because younger unrelated donor age is independently associated with improved survival after controlling for HLA compatibility
Deferring donor selection until HLA-DQB1 and -DPB1 typing is completed, because an 8/8 match at HLA-A, -B, -C, and -DRB1 is not considered an adequately matched unrelated donor in US practice
Selecting whichever donor has greater body weight relative to the recipient, because donor-recipient size mismatch is the single greatest predictor of survival among HLA-matched unrelated donors

questions.explanation_correct: Among otherwise HLA-, ABO-, and CMV-matched unrelated donor options, donor age has a demonstrated independent effect on outcome: survival is greater for recipients of grafts from younger unrelated donors (in the 18-32 year range) compared with grafts from older donors, after controlling for HLA compatibility. Between a 24-year-old and a 52-year-old otherwise-equivalent donor, the younger donor is therefore the preferred choice.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect - the source states the opposite association: survival is greater with younger unrelated donors, not older ones, after controlling for HLA compatibility.

Incorrect - KIR status is listed only as one of several additional variables considered 'in some institutions' when choosing among equivalent HLA-matched donors; it is not described as the sole or dominant determinant of survival.

Correct - donor age is explicitly identified as a factor with a positive effect on survival, with younger unrelated donors (18-32 years) outperforming older donors after controlling for HLA compatibility.

Incorrect - the source notes that because two large studies failed to show an individual impact of DQB1 mismatch on survival, it is common US practice to consider an 8/8-matched donor (HLA-A, -B, -C, -DRB1) as 'fully matched'; DQB1/DPB1 typing may refine selection but an 8/8 match is not described as inadequate.

Incorrect - greater donor size relative to the recipient is mentioned as one factor that may have a positive effect, but it is not presented as outweighing all others, and no weight data are given in this vignette to act on; it is not the factor directly supported by the comparison presented (donor age).

source_references.supports: States that donor age has a positive effect on outcome and that survival is greater for recipients of grafts from younger unrelated donors aged 18-32 years compared with older donors, after controlling for HLA compatibility; also lists gender, parity, ABO/CMV matching, size, and (in some centers) KIR status as additional donor selection variables.

Explains that because two large studies did not show an individual impact of DQB1 mismatch on survival, US centers commonly regard an 8/8 match at HLA-A, -B, -C, and -DRB1 as 'fully matched,' though DQB1/DPB1/DRB3-4-5 may be added to further prioritize donors.

group.entity: Selection of Appropriate HPC Source for a Given Patient

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with high-risk acute myeloid leukemia (AML) in first complete remission is scheduled for myeloablative allogeneic hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) transplantation from his HLA-matched sibling. He had an invasive fungal infection during prior induction chemotherapy, so the transplant team wants to minimize the duration of severe neutropenia and thrombocytopenia in the peri-transplant period. Which HPC graft source is most appropriate for this patient?

options.text: HPC-cord blood, because of its lower risk of acute GVHD
HPC-marrow, because of its lower risk of GVHD compared with HPC-apheresis
HPC-apheresis (peripheral blood stem cells), because of faster neutrophil and platelet engraftment compared with HPC-marrow
Autologous HPC collection, because it avoids the need for donor HLA matching

questions.explanation_correct: Per the comparison of graft sources, HPC-apheresis products engraft neutrophils and platelets faster than HPC-marrow. Given this patient's history of invasive fungal infection, minimizing the duration of neutropenia is a clinical priority, and he has an HLA-matched sibling available to donate an apheresis product, making HPC-apheresis the most appropriate source despite its somewhat higher GVHD risk relative to marrow.

option_rationales.text: Incorrect. HPC-cord blood carries a lower risk of acute GVHD and no risk to the donor, but it has a longer time until engraftment than the other sources, which is undesirable when rapid neutrophil/platelet recovery is the priority.

Incorrect. HPC-marrow does carry a lower GVHD risk than HPC-apheresis, but marrow is associated with slower engraftment than apheresis and marrow donation itself carries a higher risk of donor adverse events and requires anesthesia.
Correct. HPC-apheresis provides faster engraftment of neutrophils and platelets than HPC-marrow, which best addresses this patient's need to shorten the period of severe cytopenia.

Incorrect. Malignant processes such as AML are typically treated with allogeneic HPC transplantation to obtain a graft-versus-leukemia effect; autologous transplantation is more typically used for lymphoproliferative or plasma cell disorders in adults or certain solid tumors in children, not as a substitute for an available HLA-matched allogeneic donor in AML.

source_references.supports: Table 19-1 lists HPC-apheresis as offering faster neutrophil and platelet engraftment than HPC-marrow but with higher GVHD risk; HPC-marrow has lower GVHD risk but higher donor adverse event risk and requires anesthesia; HPC-cord blood has lower acute GVHD risk and no donor risk but longer time to engraftment.

Malignant myeloproliferative/leukemic processes are often treated with allogeneic HPC transplantation (for graft-versus-leukemia effect), whereas autologous transplantation is more typical for lymphoproliferative or plasma cell disorders in adults or certain pediatric solid tumors.

questions.teaching_point: Selecting the appropriate HPC graft source (marrow, apheresis-derived peripheral blood stem cells, or cord blood) requires weighing the patient's clinical priorities, such as speed of engraftment, against source-specific risks like GVHD and donor morbidity.
